description: The temple sacred to the god Apollo on the Palatine Hill in Rome.

name: Aedes Fauni
description: A temple dedicated to Faunus located on Tiber Island, the Aedes Fauni was dedicated in 196 BCE by Gnaeus Domitius Ahenobarbus and Gaius Scribonius Libo.

name: Aedes Herculis Pompeiani
description: A temple of Hercules located near the Circus Maximus.

name: Aedes Salus
description: While consul in 311 BCE, Caius Iunius Bubulcus Brutus vowed the temple of Salus; he would later dedicate it as dictator in 302 BCE. While no physical remains of the temple are known, textual and epigraphic testimony suggest that it would have been located near the present-day Piazza del Quirinale.

name: Aedes Serapis
description: Caracalla dedicated a temple to Serapis in Regio VI. Its dedication is confirmed via epigraphic elements and some of its architectural elements are also known. It was located on the Collis Latiaris, which is the southern most part of the Quirinal Hill.

name: Aedes Tensarum
description: A temple on Rome's Capitoline Hill where the carts that carried the exuviae in festival parades were stored.

name: Ag. Demetrios
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 C3 Ag. Demetrios

name: Ag. Ioannis Rodakis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 C4 Ag. Ioannis Rodakis

name: Agios Elias Doric temple
description: The Doric temple at Agios Elias, above Asea in Arcadia, dates to ca. 500 B.C.

name: Agora of Halaesa
description: The agora of the ancient city of Halaesa (second century B.C.), now within the archaeological area of Alesa Arconidea, Sicily.

name: Agueneb
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 29 G3 Agueneb

name: Ain Aata
description: Sanctuary of the Roman period

name: Ain Dara temple
description: The Ain Dara temple, an archaeological site located near the village of Ain Dara, in Afrin, Syria, was the location of an Iron Age Syro-Hittite temple. The structure was noted for the similarities it bore to Solomon's Temple in Jerusalem. The site was in use from ca. 1300 until 740 BCE. It finds comparison with the temples at Emar, Mumbaqa, and Ebla's Temple D. In January 2018 the ruins of the temple were substantially damaged by air strikes carried out by the Turkish Air Force during the Afrin offensive. The site also subsequently suffered further damage, wanton destruction, and looting.

name: Ain Hersha Roman Temple
description: Ain Hersha is the site of a Roman temple located in the environs of Mount Hermon.

name: Akītu of Šarrat-Nippuri
description: Temple in Babylon, not located, but probably not far from Eḫursagtila, for it is mentioned together in the ritual texts of the "Divine Love Lyrics". Šarrat-Nippuri "The Queen of Nippur" was a hypostasis of Ištar. It is difficult to ascertain the precise reason for the existence of an Akītu temple dedicated to this deity in the city of Babylon.

name: Akītu-Temple of Ištar
description: The New Year’s House (or Akītu Temple) of Nineveh's tutelary deity Ištar appears to date back at least to the Middle Assyrian period (reign of Tukultī-Ninurta I). Its Sumerian ceremonial name is not preserved in extant cuneiform sources. This temple's exact location remains unknown and it is certain whether Ištar's Akītu Temple was located in the citadel or in the lower town.

name: Alcántara
description: The Roman temple of Alcántara is a well-preserved structure located at one side of the Alcántara Bridge in Extremadura (Spain). The building, dating to A.D. 103, is the work of Caius Iulius Lacer. The lintel inscription indicates the structure was dedicated to Trajan.

name: Allan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D5 Allan

name: Allonzier-la-Caille
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 G2 Allonzier-la-Caille

name: Altar at the temple of Amphiaraos
description: An altar located to the east of the temple of Amphiaraos.

name: Anaktoron at Pantalika
description: The Anaktoron is an enigmatic megalithic building associated with the prehistoric Sicilian settlement of Pantalica; its polygonal blocks may date to the 12-11th c. B.C., but the current form of the building seems to be the result of Byzantine reconstruction.

name: Andrianton
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 75 D2 Andrianton

name: Anemospilia
description: A Minoan temple (?) located on northern end of Mount Iuktas.

name: Angdisseon, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E4 Angdisseon, T.

name: Angkor Wat
description: The principal temple of Angkor, the capital city of the Khmer empire, Angkor Wat was constructed in the 12th century CE. Angkor was inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage site in 1992.

name: Antinoeion at Hadrian's Villa
description: A sacred precinct at the western end of Villa Hadriani, comprising two temples at opposite (north and south) ends of a plaza or garden, and (on the western edge of this complex) a small colonnaded hemicycle. Here, Hadrian may have planned to build a large, semi-circular temple-tomb for Antinous, in place of which his successors built the colonnaded hemicycle.

name: Aphrodision
description: Located outside the city walls of Pergamon, this temple, according to an inscription (OGIS 273-9), was supposedly the site of a victory of Attalos I over the Tolistogi, Galatians, and Antiochus Hierax. It was likely later destroyed by Philip V.

name: Aphrodision at Akrai
description: Temple dedicated to Aphrodite constructed in the 6th century BCE at the Greek colony of Akrai.

name: Apollo Alaeus (temple)
description: A sanctuary of Apollo near Punta Alice. Tradition ascribed its foundation to Philoctetes and it is said he dedicated his bow to Apollo.

name: Apollo Axyros, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 H4 Apollo Axyros, T.

name: Apollo Erethimios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 60 G3 Apollo Erethimios, T.

name: Apollo Korythos Sanctuary
description: A multi-phase sanctuary of Apollo that begins in the archaic period. Pausanias visited the sanctuary and observed two cult statues of the god. One of these, sacred to Apollo Corynthus, was of wood. The other, sacred to Apollo Argeotas, was of bronze.

name: Apollo Nasiotas, T.
description: A sanctuary dedicated to Apollo Nasiotas located on the islet of Apsiphia.

name: Apollo Tegyraios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 E3 Apollo Tegyraios, T.

name: Apollo Thourios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D4 Apollo Thourios, T.

name: Apollo-Grannus-Tempel
description: A Roman temple in Faimingen near Lauingen in Bavaria.

name: Apollo, Aedes
description: The likely site of the original sanctuary to Apollo at Rome. The visible remains belong to the Augustan phase.

name: Apollo, T.
description: The oracular shrine of Apollo Koropaios.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 E4 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 F3 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 G3 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 C4 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 E4 Apollo, T.

name: Apollo, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 D1 Apollo, T.

name: Aqaba
description: Aqaba - now Aaqbe - is a place located in the Beqaa Governorate in Lebanon. It is the site of a Roman temple that has been classified as belonging to the "Mount Hermon" temple group.

name: Ara della Regina
description: A fourth to third century B.C. sanctuary at Tarquinia.

name: Ara Pacis Augustae
description: The "Altar of Augustan Peace" was located in the Campus Martius of Rome. It was originally situated to the west of the Via Lata, near the eastern end of Augustus' Horologium. The reconstructed fragments of the altar are now housed in a museum located on the Tiber's banks in the Piazza Augusto Imperatore, a square created during the Fascist era in order to spotlight the ruins of the Mausoleum of Augustus.

name: Archegetes, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D4 Archegetes, T.

name: Aron
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H4 Aron

name: Artemis Diktynnaia, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D4 Artemis Diktynnaia, T.

name: Artemis Elaphebolia, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Artemis Elaphebolia, T.

name: Artemis Parthenos, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 D3 Artemis Parthenos, T.

name: Artemis Tauropolos, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 C2 Artemis Tauropolos, T.

name: Artemision
description: The possible site of the temple of Artemis Proseoa beneath the Ag. Georgios chapel, Artemision (Kourbatsi) Euboea.

name: Arthur's O'On
description: A circular Roman temple that stood on the river Carron until it was destroyed in 1743.

name: Asea Vigla
description: A Doric temple dated to the sixth century BCE.

name: Asklepieion (Kos)
description: A major, ancient sanctuary of Asklepios located on the Aegean island of Kos.

name: Asklepieion and Lerna
description: A sanctuary complex on the northern margins of ancient Corinth near the theater.

name: Athena, Zeus?, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A5 Athena, Zeus?, T.

name: Augusteum at Carthago Nova/Col. Urbs Iulia
description: A temple dedicated to the imperial cult during the reign of Augustus.

name: Ayn-Manawir Temple
description: Temple dedicated to Osiris located 3km west of the modern village of Dush.

name: Bailleul-sur-Thérain
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Bailleul-sur-Thérain

name: Balmarcodes, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Balmarcodes, T.

name: Banteay Srei
description: Buddhist temple at Angkor, located approximately 20 km northeast of the main temple complex. Dedicated to Shiva, the temple was constructed during the 10th century CE during the reigns of Rajendravarman II and Jayavarman V.

name: Barbar Temples
description: Archaeological site in northern Bahrain associated with the Dilmun culture. Three successive temples were built on the site during the 3rd-2nd millennia BCE.

name: Bard-i Nishande
description: Bard-i Nishande is the site of a ruined ancient landscape consisting of multiple complexes. According to K. Schippmann, these complexes include a ruler's residence or "castle", a terraced platform used for ritual activity, and a lower town.

name: Bassai
description: The Temple of Apollo Epikourios (often Latinized as Epicurius) at Bassae is an innovative mid-5th century B.C. Doric temple in Arcadia. The temple preserves the oldest Corinthian capital yet found. It was added to the UNESCO World Heritage List in 1986.

name: Bayon at Angkor Thom
description: A temple in the Khmer capital of Angkor Thom, constructed in the late 12th or early 13th century CE.

name: Beaulieu
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 B3 Beaulieu

name: Belvedere Temple, Orvieto
description: A fifth century B.C. temple that was likely sacred to Tinia.

name: Beni Guedal
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C2 Beni Guedal

name: Beşdut
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 86 E3 Beşdut

name: Bidoni
description: A monumental sanctuary (26 x 20 m) dedicated to Iuppiter.

name: Bir Nesma
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F3 Bir Nesma

name: Boades
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G4 Boades

name: Brauron/sanctuary of Artemis Brauronia
description: Brauron was a sacred site in Attica dedicated to Artemis Brauronia. The sanctuary was active from the Middle Bronze Age period, although a gap in activity stretches from the Final Bronze Age to the eighth century B.C.

name: Brean Down
description: Brean Down is the site of a Romano-British temple that was built ca. A.D. 340 and destroyed about 50 years later. The site was excavated between 1957-8.

name: Brides-les-Bains
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 H3 Brides-les-Bains

name: Brigstock
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G2 Brigstock

name: Brihadishwara Temple
description: 11th century Hndu temple Tanjuvar dedicated to Shiva with smaller shrines around it.

name: Bu Darag
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 C1 Bu Darag

name: Bully
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D2 Bully

name: Burj al-Qa'im
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 91 E3 Burj al-Qa'im

name: Büyük Çelebi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 A4 Büyük Çelebi

name: Bziza
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A5 Bziza

name: Caesareum of Alexandria
description: Hellenistic temple constructed by Cleopatra VII and dedicated to Julius Caesar, and later rededicated by Augustus to himself. In 13 BCE Augustus relocated two obelisks from Heliopolis to the site. The temple was converted to a Christian church in the mid-4th century.

name: Calalzo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 E3 Calalzo

name: Camp-Ferrus
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 G1 Camp-Ferrus

name: Capitolium
description: The so-called Capitolium of Aquinum is found in the northwest part of the site. It may predate Roman colonization, and it is perhaps sacred to Iuno Regina.

name: Capitolium at Mactaris
description: The Capitolium temple at Mactaris likely dates to the reign of Marcus Aurelius.

name: Capitolium at Thugga
description: The Capitolium at Thugga dates to the middle of the second century and carries epigraphic dedications to Lucius Verus and Marcus Aurelius dating to the late 160s.

name: Capitolium of Brixia
description: The Capitolium of Brixia was built in A.D. 73.

name: Capitolium of Cosa
description: The so-called Capitolium temple of the Latin colony of Cosa.

name: Capitolium of Sala
description: Tetrastyle Corinthian temple located in the Roman forum at Sala (modern Chellah) dating from the early Hadrianic period (c.120 CE).

name: Capitolium of Sarsina
description: The remains of a first-century BCE temple podium discovered at Sarsina have been identified as the Roman settlement's Capitolium.

name: Capitolium of Terracina
description: A Tuscanic podium temple that is often referred to as being of the Capitolium type was built at Terracina in the middle of the first century BCE.

name: Capitolium of Verona
description: Excavations have determined the location of the capitolium of Roman Verona, part of an extensive, terraced complex that underpins medieval and later structures of the city. The capitolium itself stretches from the northern end of the modern Piazza Erbe to the via Emilei, lying beneath the Palazzo Maffei, church of San Benedetto, Palazzo Malaspina and surrounding structures.

name: Capitolium Vetus
description: The Capitolium Vetus was located on the Quirinal Hill and was an earlier shrine to the triad of Iuppiter, Juno, and Minerva than the famous Capitoline Temple. It was likely perched on the northern edge of the Quirinal.

name: Casalbore
description: At modern Casalbore, Italy, archaeological evidence includes a significant temple structure and associated material from the Samnite and Roman periods.

name: Cave 1 at Udayagiri
description: The southernmost cave at Udayagiri, dedicated to an unidentified god.

name: Cave 13 at Udayagiri
description: Cave 13 at Udayagiri, known as the Nārāyaṇa cave, contains a large relief panel of Vishnu's Nārāyaṇa avatar.

name: Cave 19 at Udayagiri
description: The largest cave temple at Udayagiri, known as the Amrita or Viṣṇupada cave.

name: Cave 5 at Udayagiri
description: Cave 5 at Udayagiri is dedicated to Vishnu and contains a monumental relief panel of Vishnu's Varaha avatar.

name: Ceres et Faustina (temple)
description: The Roman temple dedicated to Ceres and Faustina received patronage from Herodes Atticus. Its remains were eventually repurposed in the construction of the church of Sant'Urbano.

name: Cerreto Sannita
description: A Samnite temple dedicated to Flora.

name: Cerro de los Santos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 27 D3 Cerro de los Santos

name: Chahar Deh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 A3 Chahar Deh

name: Chalencon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D4 Chalencon

name: Chanac
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 A5 Chanac

name: Chanceaux-sur-Choisille
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 F1 Chanceaux-sur-Choisille

name: Changiyeh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Changiyeh

name: Chapel of Hakor
description: Chapel for the Sacred Barque of Amun-Ra in Karnak, established by the Egyptian pharaoh Hakor of the Twenty-ninth dynasty who reigned from 392/1 to 379/8 BCE.

name: Château de Chatillon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 G1 Château de Chatillon

name: Château-Bas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 E2 Château-Bas

name: Circus Maximus Mithraeum
description: A mithraeum adjacent to the Circus Maximus and facing the carceres of the same was located at the margin of the Forum Boarium and now situated beneath a building in the via dell'Ara Massima. The site was excavated in 1931.

name: Classical Temple at Ai Lia
description: Distyle in antis temple of the fourth century BCE, built from the salvage of the large archaic temple, likely destroyed in the earthquake of 373 BCE. This temple remained in use until the first or second centuries CE.

name: Cocullo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 44 E1 Cocullo

name: Comblessac
description: A heptagonal Gallo-Roman temple.

name: Commercial Stoa of Mytilene
description: This stoa was built against a fourth century BCE seawall sometime in the Hellenistic era. The seawall shows evidence of later Roman repairs.

name: Corlier
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 E1 Corlier

name: Coste Guizon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 15 A1 Coste Guizon

name: Crepaddosso
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D3 Crepaddosso

name: Cueva del Agua
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 26 D3 Cueva del Agua

name: Cybele, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 A2 Cybele, T.

name: Cybele, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 B2 Cybele, T.

name: Cybele, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Cybele, T.

name: Darre-ye Barre
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Darre-ye Barre

name: Dea Dia (temple)
description: The Arval Brethren cultivated the cult of the Dea Dia. The remains of this Augustan era temple, located at La Tavernaccia restaurant in the Magliana Vecchia neighborhood of Rome, are subterranean and not open to the public.

name: Deir el-Shelwit
description: A small temple dedicated to Isis on West bank of the Nile at Luxor. Its ancient name is unknown.

name: Delphinion
description: A sanctuary of Apollo Delphinios in Athens, which was closely associated with Theseus and served as a law court for cases of justifiable homicide. It is perhaps to be identified with the Classical Doric temple, whose remains are located south of the Olympieion.

name: Demeter, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Demeter, T.

name: Deneuvre
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G4 Deneuvre

name: Desaignes
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 D4 Desaignes

name: Deuilly
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 D4 Deuilly

name: Dharmaraja Cave
description: Modern name of a seventh-century Pallava cave temple in Mahābalipuram.

name: Dharmaraja Ratha
description: Modern name of a seventh-century Pallava rock-cut temple in Mahābalipuram.

name: Diana Tifatina, T.
description: The Fanum Dianae Tifatinae located on the slopes of Mt. Tifata in Campania.

name: Didyma/Branchidai/Ioustinianopolis
description: The ancient sanctuary of Apollo near Miletos (modern Didim, Turkey).

name: Diktynnaion
description: A sanctuary located at Menies bay in Chania, Crete, with remains ranging in date from Hellenistic period to Late Antiquity.

name: Dimri
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 81 C2 Dimri

name: Dionysos Bresagenes, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C3 Dionysos Bresagenes, T.

name: Dionysos, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 D3 Dionysos, T.

name: Djedars
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 29 G1 Djedars

name: Domburg
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 D1 Domburg

name: Donon
description: A sanctuary of the god Mercury in the Vosges Mountains.

name: Doric temple
description: A temple of the Doric order (VIII,7,30-34) located in the so-called Triangular Forum of Pompeii.

name: Doric Temple at Kardaki
description: An Archaic Greek temple at Corfu built in the Doric order ca. 500 B.C.

name: Doric temple at Kaulonia
description: The Doric Temple at Caulonia has been dated ca. 420 B.C.

name: Doric temple of Segesta
description: The Doric temple of Segesta is an incomplete (6 x 14) temple of the Doric order begun in the 420s B.C.

name: Drion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 45 C1 Drion

name: Dún Ailinne
description: An ancient ceremonial site located in County Kildare, Ireland. The circular enclosure once covered ca. 13 ha.

name: E-ibbi-Anum
description: The temple of the god Uraš at Dilbat. The Akkadian ceremonial name E-ibbi-Anum means "House, Anu Called It into Being."

name: Ea temple (Khorsabad)
description: As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Ea.

name: Ea-Damkina temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Ea and the goddess Damkina. Although its location is not known, the Ea-Damkina temple at Kalhu is thought to have been situated within the Ninurta temple complex, in the northwest corner of the citadel.

name: Eamaškuga
description: The temple of the goddess Ningal at Kissik. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eamaškuga means "House, Pure Sheepfold."

name: Eandasaya
description: The temple of the goddess Ištar of the Star, Eandasaya (“House Which Rivals Heaven”), was located in the New City district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of two temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Eanna
description: The temple of the god Anu and the goddess Ištar at Uruk. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eanna means "House of Heaven."

name: Early Mithraeum
description: First phase of a sacred space dedicated to the worship of the god Mithras at Dura-Europos.

name: Ebabbar
description: The temple of the god Šamaš at Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ebabbar means "Shining House."

name: Ebabbar
description: The temple of the god Šamaš at Larsa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ebabbar means "Shining House."

name: Ebursasa
description: The temple of the god Šara, Ebursasa (“House of Beautiful Jars”), was located in the Bāb-Lugalerra district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Edimgalkalama
description: The temple of the god Ištarān/Anu-rabû at Der. The Sumerian ceremonial name Edimgalkalama means "House, Great Bond of the Land."

name: Eduranna
description: The ziggurat of the god Šamaš at Larsa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eduranna means "House, Bond of Heaven."

name: Egalmah
description: The temple of the goddess Gula (as Ninisinna) at Isin. The Sumerian ceremonial name Egalmah means "Exalted Palace."

name: Egipar
description: The Giparu-House at Ur was the temple of the high-priestess of the moon-god.

name: Egišhurankia
description: The temple of the goddess Bēlet-Ninūa, Egišhurankia (“House of the Ordinances of Heaven and Underworld”), was located in the Bāb-Lugalerra district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon. Egišhurankia seems to have been located near Imgur-Enlil, along the northern wall.

name: Ehilikalamma
description: The temple of the goddess Ašratu, Ehilikalamma (“House of the Luxuriance of the Land”), was located in the Ka-dingirra district (modern Sahn) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of four temples in that part of the city, which was located in the northern part of the eastern half of Babylon.

name: Ehulhul
description: The temple of the god Sîn at Harran. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ehulhul means "House which Gives Joy."

name: Ehursagtilla
description: The temple of the god Ninurta, Ehursagtilla (“House Which Exterminates the Mountains”), was located in the Šuanna district (modern Ishin Aswad) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of two temples in that part of the city, which was located in the southern part of the eastern half of Babylon. The building was rebuilt by the Neo-Babylonian king Nabopolassar (r. 625–605 BC).

name: Eigikalamma
description: The temple of the god Lugal-Marda at Marad. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eigikalamma means "House, Eye of the Land."

name: Ekadima
description: The temple of the god Belili, Ekadima (“House Which Creates ...”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Ekarzagina
description: The temple of the god Ea within the Esagil(a) complex at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekarzagina means "Pure Quay"or "Quay of Lapis Lazuli."

name: Ekinu
description: The temple of the goddess Aya at Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekinu means "House, Bed-Chamber."

name: Ekišnugal
description: The temple of the moon god Nanna/Suen/Sîn at Ur from the third to first millennium B.C. Over the course of its long history, it was rebuilt (and expanded) numerous times. A few of its more famous builders were Narām-Sîn of Agade, Ur-Nammu of Ur, and Nebuchadnezzar II of Babylon.

name: Ekišnugal
description: The temple of the god Sîn, Ekišnugal (“House of the Great Light”), was located in the Kullab district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of four temples in that part of Babylon. The temple is mentioned in Old Babylonian Period texts and was (re)built by the Old Babylonian king Abī-ēšuh (r. ca. 1711–1684 BC) and the Neo-Babylonian ruler Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC). The temple survived into the Hellenistic Period.

name: Ekitušgarza
description: The temple of the goddess Bēlet-Eanna, Ekitušgarza (“House, Abode of the Regulations”), was located in the Tuba district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon. Ekitušgarza was rebuilt by the Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–652 BC).

name: Ekitušgirzal
description: The temple of the goddess Bēlet-Eanna, Ekitušgirzal (“House, Abode of Joy”), was located in the New City district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of two temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Ekituškuga
description: A ziggurat dedicated to the goddess Ištar was built near the center of the citadel of Nineveh, not far from the city’s main temple Emašmaš. This structure was given the Sumerian ceremonial names Ekituškuga ("House, Pure Abode") and Ekibikuga ("House whose Site is Pure").

name: Ekunankuga
description: The ziggurat of the god Šamaš at Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekunankuga means "House, Pure Stairway of Heaven."

name: Ekurra
description: The temple of the god Bunene at Sippar. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ekurra means "House of the Mountains."

name: el Qassabat el-Gharbiya
description: An ancient place, cited: None

name: el-Chatatba
description: A ruined temple built in Cipollino marble located about 10 km south of Sabratha.

name: el-Garrusc
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 37 B1 el-Garrusc

name: el-Hosn
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 C4 el-Hosn

name: el-Muftella
description: An ancient place, cited: None

name: el-Nadura
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 79 D3 el-Nadura

name: el-Qasr
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 71 B3 el-Qasr

name: Elephant tomb at Carmona
description: Structure in the the necropolis of Carmona (near Seville) named for a small sculpture of an elephant found inside it. Rather than a tomb, it may have been a sanctuary or temple.

name: Ellès
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 33 D1 Ellès

name: Elugalgalgasisa
description: The ziggurat of the god Sîn at Ur. The Sumerian ceremonial name Elugalgalgasisa means "House of the King who Lets Counsel Flourish". The platform temple was originally built during the Early Bronze Age and was then restored in the sixth century B.C. by King Nabonidus.

name: Emah
description: The temple of the goddess Ninmah/Bēlet-ilī, Emah (“Exalated House”), was located in the Ka-dingirra district (modern Kasr) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of four temples in that part of the city, which was located in the northern part of the eastern half of Babylon. Emah was rebuilt by the Assyrian king Ashurbanipal (r. 668–ca. 631 BC) and the Babylonian king Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC).

name: Emašdari
description: The temple of the goddess Ištar of Agade at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Emašdari means "House of Animal Offerings." The Neo-Babylonian king Nabonidus rebuilt/renovated it.

name: Emašmaš
description: The most important temple in the citadel of Nineveh was dedicated to the city's tutelary deity, the goddess Ištar. The temple was located in the center of the citadel and went by the Sumerian ceremonial name Emašmaš (meaning uncertain).

name: Emekiliburur
description: The temple of the goddess Šarrat-Larsa, Emekiliburur (“House which Gathers All the Me’s”), was located in the Kullab district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of four temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Emesikila
description: The temple of the god Amurru, Emesikila (“House of Pure Me’s”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon. Emesikila is mentioned in Old Babylonian sources.

name: Emeslam
description: The temple of the god Nergal at Cutha. The Sumerian ceremonial name Emeslam means "House, Warrior of the Netherworld."

name: Emeurur
description: The temple of the goddess Nanāya, Emeurur (“House which Gathers the Me’s”), was located in the TE.E (read as either Tê or Kisīri) district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was the only temple in that part of Babylon.

name: Enamhe
description: The temple of the god Adad, Enamhe (“House of Plenty”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon. Enamhe was (re)built by the the Old Babylonian kings Sūmû-lā-El (r. ca. 1881–1845 BC) and Hammu-rāpi (r. ca. 1792–1750 BC) and the Neo-Babylonian ruler Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–562 BC).

name: Enamhe
description: The temple of the gods Adad and Apla-Adad at Anat (Anatho). The Sumerian ceremonial name Enamhe means "House of Plenty."

name: Enamtila
description: The temple of the god Bēl-mātāti (Enlil), Enamtila (“House of Life”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon. Enamtila is mentioned in Old Babylonian and Kassite Period texts.

name: Eniggidrukalamasuma
description: The temple of the god Nabû of the ḫarû at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eniggidrukalamasuma means "House which Bestows the Scepter of the Land."

name: Enlil temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Enlil. Although its location is not known, the Enlil temple at Kalhu is thought to have been situated within the Ninurta temple complex, in the northwest corner of the citadel.

name: Enunmah
description: The ḫilṣu-building of the goddess Ningal inside the Egišnugal temple at Ur. The Sumerian ceremonial name Enunmah means "House of the Exalted Prince."

name: Enunmah
description: The temple of the god Nusku, Enunmah (“House of the Exalted Prince”), was located in the Bāb-Lugalerra district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Epoissum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 F3 Epoissum

name: Ermita de S. Valero
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F4 Ermita de S. Valero

name: es-Soumaa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 31 F4 es-Soumaa

name: Esabad
description: The temple of the goddess Gula, Esabad (“House of the Open Ear”), was located in the Tuba district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon. Esabad was rebuilt by the Assyrian king Ashurbanipal (r. 668–ca. 631 BC) and Nebuchadnezzar II (r. 604–652 BC). The temple survived into Arsacid times (247 BC–AD 224).

name: Esag
description: The temple of the god Lugalbanda, Esag (“Foremost House”), was located in the Kullab district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of four temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Esagila
description: The temple of the god Marduk at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Esagil(a) means "House, Whose Top is High."

name: Ešahula
description: The temple of the god Nergal at Me-Turnat. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ešahula means "House of the Happy Heart."

name: Ešahulezenzagmukam
description: Around 690 B.C., the Neo-Assyrian king Sennacherib started construction on a New Year’s Temple for Nineveh’s tutelary deity Ištar. Ešahulezenzagmukam, whose Sumerian ceremonial name means "House of Joy and Gladness for the Festival of the Beginning of the Year" was never completed and work on it appears to have come to a complete stop ca. 688 B.C., when Sennacherib decided to rebuild the Akītu Temple at Ashur instead.

name: Ešasurra
description: The temple of the god Išhara, Ešasurra (“House of the Womb”), was located in the Šuanna district (modern Ishin Aswad) of Babylon. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, this was one of two temples in that part of the city, which was located in the southern part of the eastern half of Babylon.

name: Ešiddukišara
description: The temple of the god Nabû, Ešiddukišara (“House of the Director of the Universe”), was located in the Tuba district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of three temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Esterzili
description: The Domu de Orgia at Esterzili is the largest nuragic "megaron" structure thus far discovered in Sardinia.

name: Estrées-St-Denis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 C3 Estrées-St-Denis

name: Etemenanki
description: The ziggurat of the god Marduk at Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial name Etemenanki means "House, Foundation Platform of Heaven and Underworld."

name: Etruscan acropolis at Volaterrae
description: The multi-phase acropolis of Etruscan Volaterrae has numerous important phases, beginning in the Archaic period. The monumental site continued to develop and grow during the Hellenistic period. It was identified archaeologically only in the nineteenth century. In the twentieth century it was the subject of major investigations under M. Cristofani and, later, M. Bonamici.

name: Eulmaš
description: The temple of the goddess Anunnītu at Sippar-Anunnītu (=Sippar-Amnanum).

name: Eurguba
description: The temple of the god Pisangunuk, Eurguba (“House, Established Foundation”), was located in the Kullab district of Babylon, in the eastern half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of four temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Eurmeiminanki
description: The ziggurat of the god the god Nabû at Borsippa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Eurmeiminanki means "House which Gathers the Seven (Me's) of Heaven and Underworld."

name: Ezida
description: The temple of the god Marduk as Tutu, later of the god Nabû, at Borsippa. The Sumerian ceremonial name Ezida means "True House."

name: Ezida
description: One of the temples in the citadel of Nineveh that was dedicated to Nabû, the god of scribes; it is located north of the temple of the goddess Ištar (Emašmaš). The Sumerian ceremonial name Ezida means "True House."

name: Ezida (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the god Nabû. Unlike most of the religious buildings constructed at Kalhu, the Ezida ("True House") was built in the southeast corner of the Citadel.

name: Eʾesirkalama
description: The temple of the god Pisangunuk, Eʾesirkalama (“House of the Street of the Land”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Eʾešmah
description: The temple of the god Ea, Eʾešmah (“Exalted House”), was located in the Kumar district of Babylon, in the western half of the inner city. According to the Babylonian topographical text Tintir = Babylon Tablet IV, it was one of seven temples in that part of Babylon.

name: Fakhrikah
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 H4 Fakhrikah

name: Fanum Pithiviers-le-Vieil
description: The Fanum of Pithiviers-le-Vieil.

name: Farley Heath
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 G3 Farley Heath

name: Farrashband 1
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Farrashband 1

name: Farrashband 2
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Farrashband 2

name: Farrashband 3
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Farrashband 3

name: Farrashband 4
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Farrashband 4

name: Farrashband 5
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Farrashband 5

name: Faverolles
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 C2 Faverolles

name: Fines
description: A Gallo-Roman site lying along the Roman road from Lyon to Saintes.

name: Five Rathas
description: The Five Rathas is the modern name of a group of five seventh-century Pallava rock-cut temples at Mahābalipuram.

name: Fons Clitumni
description: Originally the site of a shrine dedicated to the river god Clitumnus and later a paleochristian shrine. The temple was listed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2011.

name: Fontans
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 A4 Fontans

name: Forggensee
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 C2 Forggensee

name: Freienbach
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 A2 Freienbach

name: Friars Wash Roman Temple
description: The Friars Wash Roman Temple was noted first in 1965. Aerial photography and subsequent archaeological investigation identified a system of ditches and two rectangular structures that were considered to be Romano-British temples.

name: Funerary chapel of Amenirdis I
description: Funerary chapel of the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Amenirdis I, daughter of King Kashta.

name: Funerary chapel of Ankhnesneferibre
description: Presumed remains of the funerary chapel of the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Ankhnesneferibre.

name: Funerary chapel of Shepenupet I
description: Funerary chapel of the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Shepenupet I, daughter of King Osorkon III.

name: Funerary chapel of Shepenupet II, Nitocris I and Mehytenweskhet
description: Funerary chapel of the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Shepenupet II, daughter of King Piankhi. Enlarged by her successor, the Divine Adoratrice of Amun Nitocris I, daughter of King Psamtik I, to accommodate her cult and that of her mother Mehytenweskhet.

name: Funerary temple of Amenhotep II
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Amenhotep II located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Funerary temple of Ay and Horemheb
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of Kings Ay and Horemheb located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Funerary temple of Seti I
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Seti I located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Funerary temple of Siptah and Twosret
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Siptah and the Queen Twosret located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Funerary temple of Thutmose III
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Thutmose III located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Funerary temple of Thutmose IV
description: Temple dedicated to the cult of King Thutmose IV located on the west bank of Thebes.

name: Furzol
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Furzol

name: Gabrias
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 A4 Gabrias

name: Gallo-Roman temple at Nasium
description: Archaeological remains of the Gallo-Roman temple at ancient Nasium, located at Mazeroie, commune Naix-aux-Forges

name: Gallo-Roman temple at Vannes
description: Remains of a Gallo-Roman at Vannes.

name: Gallo-Roman temple of Forêt d'Halatte (cne. Ognon, Oise)
description: A Gallo-Roman temple built ca. mid-first century AD that remained in use until the fifth century.

name: Gamaches-en-Vexin
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B3 Gamaches-en-Vexin

name: Ganesh Ratha
description: Modern name of a seventh-century Pallava rock-cut temple at Mahābalipuram.

name: Garian
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Garian

name: Gasr Bu Zayd
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 Gasr Bu Zayd

name: Gasr el-Gezira
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E2 Gasr el-Gezira

name: Gasr Zeitun
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 38 D1 Gasr Zeitun

name: Geran
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Geran

name: Geronisos
description: A small island lying off the west coast of Cyprus where a sanctuary has been identified.

name: Ghulbiyan
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 98 F3 Ghulbiyan

name: Giado
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E3 Giado

name: Gilan Gharbi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 A2 Gilan Gharbi

name: Glale
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 89 E3 Glale

name: Gola di S. Andrea, Itri
description: Located along the course of the Via Appia between Fondi and Itri are the remains of a likely sanctuary of Apollo that dates to the fourth and third century BCE.

name: Görresburg
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Görresburg

name: Görtschach
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 E3 Görtschach

name: Great Temple of Hera
description: The Great Temple of Hera, or Polycrates Temple, is the third temple constructed at the Heraion of Samos between 540 and 530 BCE.

name: Great Temple of Petra
description: The Great Temple of Petra is one of the major archaeological and architectural components of central Petra. It is located to the south of the Colonnaded Street and southeast of the Temenos Gate and covers 7560 square meters.

name: Greek Temple at Taras
description: A Doric temple dating to the sixth century B.C., located in the modern piazza Castello in Taranto, Italy. It was initially attributed to Poseidon, but may have been dedicated to a different deity.

name: Gula temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the goddess Gula. Although its location is not known, the Gula temple at Kalhu is thought to have been part of the Ninurta temple complex (in the northwest corner of the citadel), east of the Šarrat-niphi temple and north of the Kidmuri temple.

name: Gur-i Dukhtar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B4 Gur-i Dukhtar

name: Gura Dobrogei
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 22 F4 Gura Dobrogei

name: Gyroulas
description: A Late Archaic rural sanctuary of Demeter and Apollo at Gyroulas on Naxos.

name: Hajjiabad
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C3 Hajjiabad

name: Hapsasieion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 A2 Hapsasieion

name: Harlow
description: Harlow is the site of a pre-Roman and Roman sanctuary.

name: Harran el-Awamid
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 E3 Harran el-Awamid

name: Hathor Temple, Timna Valley
description: A shrine sacred to Hathor connected with the Egyptian mining settlement that was extracting copper in the Timna Valley.

name: Hecate, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Hecate, T.

name: Hekateion
description: The Hekateion is a monumental temple for the goddess Hekate constructed in Lagina ca. 125 BCE.

name: Henchir Krebita
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 B1 Henchir Krebita

name: Henchir Oum el-Abbas
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 B1 Henchir Oum el-Abbas

name: Henchir Oum Iala
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 F3 Henchir Oum Iala

name: Henchir Snem
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 C2 Henchir Snem

name: Hephaistion
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 65 D5 Hephaistion

name: Hera temple
description: The so-called 'Tavole Palatine' is the site of the ruins of a sixth century B.C. Doric temple sacred to Hera and associated with the city of Metapontum.

name: Herakles, T.
description: A sanctuary of Herakles located on the island of Lemnos, active from the Archaic period through to the time of the Roman empire.

name: Hermai
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 D3 Hermai

name: Hermel
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B5 Hermel

name: Hibbariye
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C3 Hibbariye

name: Hieron (Chalkedonion)/Zeus Ourios, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 53 B2 Hieron (Chalkedonion)/Zeus Ourios, T.

name: Hochscheid
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 H3 Hochscheid

name: Hockwold
description: A Romano-Celtic temple site dating between the second and fourth centuries AD.

name: Hosion kai Dikaion, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 C3 Hosion kai Dikaion, T.

name: Housesteads Mithraeum
description: Temple of Mithras at Roman fort of Housesteads (Vercovicium)

name: Husn Niha
description: Husn Niha is the site of four Roman temples that were constructed between the first and third centuries AD.

name: Husn Sfiri
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B5 Husn Sfiri

name: Hyperteleaton
description: Sanctuary of Apollo Hyperteleatas, partially excavated in 1885 and 1968. In ancient literature the sanctuary is only mentioned by Pausanias, in whose time it was called the Hyperteleaton and was dedicated to Asclepius. The site is located south of Phoiniki in the southeastern Peloponnese at a place known in the 19th century called Chasanaga.

name: Idomene Minor
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 D3 Idomene Minor

name: Ishtar Temple at Mari
description: Temple dedicated to the goddess Ishtar located in the western section of the city of Mari.

name: Italic temple of Castel di Ieri
description: The remains of an Italic temple, in the locality known as "Pié di Francia". The sanctuary dates from the fourth to first centuries B.C.

name: Kalhu ziggurat
description: The temple-tower of the god Ninurta, which was started by the ninth-century-BC Assyrian ruler Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) and completed by his son and successor Shalmaneser III (r. 858–824 BC), is still the most prominent feature of Kalhu (modern Nimrud). This multi-staged ziggurat, whose remains are now a pyramid of eroding mud-brick, was constructed in the northwest corner of the citadel, next to Kalhu's principal sanctuary, the Ninurta temple.

description: A Doric-order temple, hexastyle in antis (23.20x11.10 m). The building utilizes stone from both Aegina and Paros.

description: An unexcavated Old Kingdom temple, attested by epigraphic finds at Abydos in Egypt. Its remains are presumed to underlie later Osiris sanctuary structures and part of the modern town.

name: Khorsabad ziggurat
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had a temple-tower built on the palace terrace of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). The ziggurat was located at the northern end of the temple sector, which was accessible from Sargon’s palace, through the Courtyard XV.

name: Khortata

name: Kidmuri Temple
description: The bīt Kidmuri is one of the temples located in the citadel of Nineveh. Its Sumerian ceremonial name is not preserved in extant cuneiform sources. Although the temple's exact location remains unknown, it is generally believed to have been between the temple of the goddess Ištar (Emašmaš) and the Citadel Gate (East Gate).

name: Kidmuri temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. One of those was dedicated to the divine Kidmuri (probably a collective name for a group of gods).

name: Kiosk of Qertassi
description: The so-called Kiosk of Qertassi is an unfinished Roman kiosk that may date to the reign of Trajan. Originally located at Qertassi, the remains of the temple were relocated to New Kalabsha in Southern Egypt as part of the International Campaign to Save the Monuments of Nubia in the twentieth century.

name: Kiratah
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Kiratah

name: Klaros

name: Koçak
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 D3 Koçak

name: Koi Krylgan Kala
description: Koi Krylgan Kala is an archaeological site in Uzbekistan. At the site are the remains of a temple complex associated with the Chorasmian Dynasty, which was destroyed approximately 200 BC.

name: Kom el-Nana
description: Area south of the main city of Amarna that was the site of a temple during the Amarna period. The structure was overbuilt by a Christian monastery in late antiquity.

name: Komosarue
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 87 M2 Komosarue

name: Kordin III
description: Megalithic temple in Malta constructed during the Mġarr phase (ca. 3700 BCE) and abandoned ca. 2500 BCE.

name: Lydney
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 8 E3 Lydney

name: Madhukeshwara temple

name: Magoula Kastri

name: Mahabodhi Temple
description: One of the four main Buddhist pilgrimage sites, identified as the place where Gautama Buddha attained Enlightenment. The first temple on the site was built by Emperor Asoka in the third century B.C., and the present temple dates from the fifth or sixth centuries. It is one of the earliest Buddhist temples built entirely in brick, still standing in India, from the late Gupta period. The site was largely abandoned from the twelfth-eighteenth centuries. It was named a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2002.

name: Maimeh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Maimeh

name: Maison Carrée
description: A well-preserved Roman temple built ca. 16 B.C. and dedicated to Caius and Lucius Caesar.

name: Malchittu nuragic temple
description: A sacred structure with an 'in antis' plan located near Arzachena in Sardinia.

name: Malla
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 H4 Malla

name: Manglisi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 88 C3 Manglisi

name: Maqam er-Rab
description: A peripteral temple built of basalt that includes an altar to Nemesis. Epigraphic data provides a chronology of late second to third centuries A.D.

name: Maqsudabad
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Maqsudabad

name: Marino Mithraeum
description: A Mithraic shrine of unusual size discovered at modern Marino, Italy in 1963.

name: Markesi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 55 G3 Markesi

name: Mars, Aedes
description: A temple sacred to Mars located on the Via Appia. The shrine was located between the first and second milestones and was dedicated in the fourth century BC. According to Livy, Titus Quinctius dedicated the temple on 1 June 387 BC.

name: Mavrovounoi
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 72 C3 Mavrovounoi

name: Mdoukha
description: Roman sanctuary with two small temples.

name: Mechel

name: Megaloi Theoi (sanctuary, Imbros)
description: A sanctuary of the "Great Gods" on the Aegean island of Imbros/Gökçeada. The remains of the sanctuary are located southwest of modern Kaleköy, to the west of the airport in the vicinity of a ruined monastery of Agios Konstantinos at a place formerly known as Roxado.

name: Menelaion
description: An ancient Greek sanctuary of Helen and Menelaos near ancient Therapnai in Greece.

name: Mercurius, Aedes
description: A temple dedicated to Mercury and Maia that was located on the slopes of the Aventine Hill overlooking the Circus Maximus.

name: Meter Theon 1, T.

description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 25 F1 Mézin

name: Military Zeus Temple

name: Minatiacum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 E3 Minatiacum

name: Minerva temple at Punta della Campanella
description: An ancient temple of Minerva, located on the ancient cape that also was known by the goddess' name.

name: Minor Acropolis, Norba
description: The so-called Minor Acropolis is located on the hill to the south of the Porta Maggiore. This area is surrounded by the urban enclosure wall and is terraced in polygonal masonry. Two platform temples of unknown assignation are to be found here.

name: Mithraeum at Mundelsheim
description: A second-century Mithraeum is located in the area of the municipality of Mundelsheim in the Ludwigsburg district of Baden-Württemberg, Germany.

name: Mithraeum at Riegel
description: A temple once sacred to Mithras located in Riegel am Kaiserstuhl (Baden-Wuerttemberg, Germany).

name: Mithraeum at Segontium
description: A mithraeum was excavated at Segontium (modern Caernarfon) in 1959, ca. 137m from the east corner of the Roman auxiliary fort. The site was abandoned in the late fourth century A.D.

name: Mithraeum at Vindovala
description: A temple sacred to Mithras located at the Roman fort of Vindovala (Rudchester) on Hadrian's Wall.

name: Mithraeum of Lucretius Menander
description: The Mithraeum of Lucretius Menander located in ancient Ostia dates to the third century CE.

name: Möhn
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G3 Möhn

name: Mollis
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 B2 Mollis

name: Mont-Rivel
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 18 C3 Mont-Rivel

name: Monte Torre Maggiore
description: An Italic temple complex in Umbria of the first millennium B.C. that includes the so-called "Ara maior".

name: Montreuil-Bellay
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 E1 Montreuil-Bellay

name: Mortuary Temple of Amenhotep III
description: Temple constructed in the Theban Necropolis during the 18th Dynasty.

name: Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut
description: The Mortuary Temple of Hatshepsut at Deir el-Bahari was built for the Eighteenth dynasty Pharaoh Hatshepsut.

name: Mortuary temple of Mentuhotep II
description: The Mortuary temple of the Eleventh Dynasty pharaoh Mentuhotep II at Deir el-Bahari would inspire the later mortuary complexes of Hatshepsut and Thutmose III that are also found at the site.

name: Msellata
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 F2 Msellata

name: Muk
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C4 Muk

name: Musawwarat

name: Nabû temple (Khorsabad)
description: The eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) had a temple to the god Nabû, the god of scribes, built in the citadel of his new capital, Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad). Building H is the largest temple in Sargon’s newly-constructed city and it was built on a platform in the western side of the citadel, rather than on the palace terrace. The Nabû temple was connected to the temple district, which formed part of Sargon’s royal residence, by a stone bridge.

name: Nagos
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 C4 Nagos

name: Naisar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G3 Naisar

name: Naqsh-i Bahram
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B3 Naqsh-i Bahram

name: Natanz
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 G3 Natanz

name: Naudaran
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 C5 Naudaran

name: Navish
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 F2 Navish

name: Nebi Safa
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Nebi Safa

name: Nekromanteion
description: An ancient sanctuary of necromancy sacred to Hades and Persephone located on the banks of the Acheron River in Epirus, near the ancient city of Ephyra.

name: Nettleton
description: A significant cult center at Nettleton Shrub in Wiltshire was dedicated to Apollo Cunomaglus.

name: Neuvy-sur-Barangeon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H1 Neuvy-sur-Barangeon

name: New Year's Festival House at Assur
description: As part of his post-689-BC religious reforms, the Neo-Assyrian king Sennacherib (704–681 BC) had a new New Year’s festival house (akītu-house) constructed outside the western wall of Assur. This was an attempt to replicate the cultic topography of Babylon. The Sumerian ceremonial names of that holy building and its cella are Eabbaugga ("House Where Tiāmat Is Put To Death") and Edubdubabba ("House That Makes Tiāmat Tremble”) respectively.

name: Neyrac-les-Bains
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 C4 Neyrac-les-Bains

name: Ningal temple (Khorsabad)
description: As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the goddess Ningal.

name: Ninurta temple (Kalhu)
description: When Kalhu (biblical Calah, modern Nimrud) became the principal administrative center of the Assyrian Empire in the ninth century BC, king Ashurnasirpal II (r. 883–859 BC) built (or rebuilt) numerous temples. The most important of those was dedicated to the god Ninurta, the patron deity of city, which Ashurnasirpal had constructed between his palace and the ziggurat, a building also consecrated to Ninurta.

name: Ninurta temple (Khorsabad)
description: As part of the palatial complex of Dūr-Šarrukīn (modern Khorsabad), the eighth-century-BC Assyrian king Sargon II (r. 721–705 BC) constructed six temples, including one to the god Ninurta.

name: Nubian Lion Temple at Naqa
description: The Temple of Apedemak, also known as the Lion Temple, is located to the west of the temple of Amun.

name: Nymphaion/Balaneion Tiberinon
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 67 B4 Nymphaion/Balaneion Tiberinon

name: Odry

name: Orsingen
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 19 A2 Orsingen

name: Palaio Pretorio

name: Panóias

name: Paptoulis?

name: Parembole?

name: Parthenon

name: Passy
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 17 H2 Passy

name: Patriacum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 H2 Patriacum

name: Pecë
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 B2 Pecë

name: Pelm
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 G2 Pelm

name: Per-Ptah
description: The site of Per-Ptah, meaning "House of Ptah", lies some 90 km south of Aswan and received a temple dedicated to pharaoh Ramesses II. Some of the architectural and sculptural remains of the temple were dismantled and relocated to the site of New Kalabsha in the 1960s during the construction of the Aswan High Dam.

name: Pera Milous
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 58 G2 Pera Milous

name: Pergamos

The island was home to a multi-period temple complex, which was moved to the nearby island of Agilkia and declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in the 1970s.

description: The extraurban sanctuary of Minerva at Portonaccio is an important Etruscan cult center. The temple dates ca. 510 B.C. and was adorned with acroterial sculptures, notably the famous "Apollo of Veii".

name: Poseidon Phytalmios?, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A4 Poseidon Phytalmios?, T.

name: Poseidon?, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 61 A3 Poseidon?, T.

name: Poseidon?, T.

name: Potamos, T.

name: Pouzauges
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 14 E2 Pouzauges

name: Prdejci

name: Pre-Erechtheion

name: Preah Khan

name: Preah Ko
description: Temple constructed in the city of Hariharalaya, dedicated in 880 CE by the Khmer king Indravarman I.

name: Prepelaion?
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 56 E5 Prepelaion?

name: Psaropyrgos
description: An ancient temple of Poseidon, known today as the "Tomb of Homer", located on a height at the northern end of the Aegean island of Ios. Some funerary epigraphy has also been noted at the site.

name: Punta della Vipera Etruscan temple
description: A coastal Etruscan sanctuary that dates ca. 540-530 B.C. with additional architectural phases dating to the fourth through second centuries B.C.

name: Pusht-i Arisht
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 92 B3 Pusht-i Arisht

name: Pyra
description: Pyra is a place with preserved ruins of a Doric temple dating to the third century B.C. The sources connect Pyra with the pyre of the hero Herakles.

name: Pyramid-complex of Senusret III
description: Funerary complex of King Senusret III at Dahshur including a pyramid.

name: Qadamgah
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 D4 Qadamgah

name: Qadboun
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 B3 Qadboun

name: Qafr Zebad
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 C2 Qafr Zebad

name: Qalaat Faqra
description: A Roman-era sanctuary complex in Lebanon with multiple altars and two temples. On the basis of epigraphic evidence, one of these temples seems to have been dedicated to Atargatis and the other possibly to Zeus Beelgalassos. There is some evidence for worship also of Zeus Heliopolis and for the Roman imperial cult. A Byzantine basilica was later built on the site.

name: Qanat-i Bagh
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 94 B4 Qanat-i Bagh

name: Qasr el-Aguz
description: Site in Luxor (Egypt) associated with a Ptolemaic temple dedicated to the god Thoth.

name: Qasr el-Banat
description: The remains of an ancient temple at Qasr el Banat, Lebanon,

name: Qasr el-Sagha temple
description: Temple located north of Lake Qarun in the Fayyum Depression. Tentatively dated to the Old Kingdom period, the temple may have been dedicated to the god Sobek.

name: Qasr Naous
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 68 A5 Qasr Naous

name: Qasr Neba
description: Qasr Neba is the site of a Roman temple in the Bekaa valley of Lebanon.

name: Qasr Nemrud
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 69 D2 Qasr Nemrud

name: Quarrush
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 37 B1 Quarrush

name: Raiano
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 42 F4 Raiano

name: Ramappa Temple
description: A thirteenth-century temple that was built during the period of the Kakatiya ruler Ganapati Deva.

name: Ramesseum
description: The funerary temple of Ramesses II, built within the area known as the Ipet-isut, "The Most Selected of Places", along the Nile River.

name: Ras il-Wardija
description: A Punic-Roman sanctuary site located on the western coast of Gozo (Gaulos), Malta.

name: Re-Buried Hellenistic Temple and Workshops
description: Excavations on site of post office at 49 Ben Ami St. revealed Hellenistic remains, including a dedication to Zeus Soter on behalf of Antiochos VII by a courtier (SEG XIX 9094). This inscription was associated with Hellenistic architecture of a temple-like character. Nearby workshops also uncovered.

name: Resras
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 80 B4 Resras

name: Ribemont-sur-Ancre
description: Ribemont-sur-Ancre is the site of a Celtic and Gallo-Roman sanctuary located on the right bank of the Ancre river, a tributary of the Somme. Roger Agache discovered the site in 1962. The site is vast in its area, covering some 80 ha.

name: Rijelat Umm Kubar
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 91 C4 Rijelat Umm Kubar

name: Rijsbergen
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 E1 Rijsbergen

description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 54 A2 Roda

name: Rogeban
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 35 E3 Rogeban
