description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 A4 Zeus Sabazios, T.

name: Zeus Thenatas, T.
description: Zeus Thenatas, T. is an archaic Greek sanctuary built atop earlier Minoan cult remains. The open-air temple remained in use until the second century AD.

name: Zeus Thimenos, T.
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 62 A3 Zeus Thimenos, T.

name: Zeus, T.
description: The temple of Zeus Ikmaios on Keos.

name: Zingsheim
description: A Gallo-Roman temple of the Matronae Aufaniae.

name: ‘Lavobrinta’?
description: ‘Lavobrinta’? was a Roman fort on the east bank of the Severn river.

name: "Western Theater" at Laodicea ad Lycum
description: Digitized by eye in Google Earth on 13 July 2012 at an eye altitude of 683m. Dates are general settlement dates, following Foss and Mitchell in BAtlas.

name: [Augusta] Ambianorum
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B2 [Augusta] Ambianorum

name: Acharnes ancient theater
description: The deme theater of Acharnai was discovered in 2007.

name: Agbia amphitheater
description: A Roman amphitheater located at Agbia in what is now Tunisia (Henchir Ain Hedja).

name: Alcazaba de Villavieja
description: A Roman amphitheater at Berja.

name: Aldborough Studforth Hill
description: Archaeological remains at 'Aldborough Studforth Hill' site may be the location of possible Roman amphitheater.

name: Amphitheater at Castrum Rauracense
description: Discovered in late 2021 on the site of the Basler Ruderclub in Augst, this amphitheater lies just west of the Roman fort of Castrum Rauracense. It seems to have been built in an abandoned quarry sometime in the 4th century CE.

name: Amphitheater Birten
description: A Roman amphitheater located at Castra Vetera (modern Birten Germany)

name: Amphitheater of Alba Fucens
description: A first century A.D. amphitheater at Alba Fucens built during the reign of Tiberius.

name: Amphitheater of Anazarbos
description: A Roman amphitheater located south and outside the city walls at Anazarbos (modern Dilekkaya in Turkey).

name: Amphitheater of Dura-Europos
description: Intramural amphitheater located within the military sector on the north side of Dura-Europos. Built on the site of the former Bath F3, which was destroyed by fire. The amphitheater's dedication in 216 CE is attested by an inscription found fallen from above entrance C on the south side of the amphitheater.

name: Amphitheater of Ercavica
description: A Roman amphitheater located in Cañaveruelas, Spain.

name: Amphitheater of Micia
description: The Amphitheater of Micia.

name: Amphitheater of Pompeii
description: The Amphitheater of Pompeii is the earliest known surviving example of a Roman amphitheater. It was built between 80 and 70 B.C.

name: Amphitheater of Sbeitla
description: The Roman Amphitheater of Sbeitla.

name: Amphitheater of the Three Gauls
description: The Sanctuary of the Three Gauls (Tres Galliae) was a late first century B.C. sanctuary in Lugdunum; the amphitheater dates to 19 B.C.

name: Amphitheater of Thizica
description: The small Roman amphitheater of Thizica.

name: Amphithéâtre Gallo-Romain
description: The ruins of the Roman amphitheater Saint-André-sur-Cailly are located in the hamlet of Bout Levé.

name: Amphitheatre of El Jem
description: Third century A.D. Roman amphitheater; declared a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1979.

name: Amphitheatre of Thapsus
description: A stone-built Amphitheatre at Thapsus.

name: Amphitheatrum Castrense
description: The Amphitheatrum Castrense is a Roman amphitheater built in the third century AD. Its structure was later incorporated into the Aurelianic Walls. Adjacent to the amphitheater is the Christian church of Santa Croce in Gerusalemme.

name: Amphitheatrum Flavium
description: The Flavian amphitheater at Rome (the so-called "Colosseum").

name: Ancient Greek theater at Palazzolo Acreide
description: The theater at ancient Akrai.

name: Ancient Theater of Amphilochian Argos
description: An ancient theater located at Amphilochian Argos. There are traces of the theater to be found at Ag. Ioannis near Neokhori in Aitoloakarnania, West Greece.

name: Ancient Theater of Argos
description: The Ancient Theater of Argos was a Hellenistic theater initially constructed in 320 BCE and later renovated by the Romans.

name: ancient theater of Carthaea
description: The ancient theater of Carthaea.

name: Ancient Theater of Chaeronea
description: The theater at Chaeronea

name: Ancient theater of Eretria
description: The Ancient Greek theater of Eretria is located inside the so-called West Gate of the city walls in the northwest sector of the city. It dates from ca. 400 B.C. to ca. 150 B.C.

name: Ancient theater of Kourion
description: The Ancient theater of Kourion was first built in the second century B.C. It remained in use until the fourth century.

name: Ancient theater of Maroneia
description: The ancient theater of Maroneia was constructed in the Hellenistic period and later remodeled in the Roman period.

name: Ancient Theater of Teos
description: Originally constructed during the Hellenistic period, the theater at Teos was extensively modified during the early Roman empire.

name: Anfiteatro di Bleso
description: The "Anfiteatro di Bleso" is a second century A.D. Roman amphitheater at Tibur (modern Tivoli in Italy).

name: Anfiteatro Fausto
description: The "Anfiteatro Fausto" or "Amphitheater of Faustus" refers to a Roman amphitheater that was built during the reign of Tiberius (likely 30 or 32 CE) at Terni.

name: Anfiteatro laterizio
description: A Roman amphitheater located to the northwest of Nola and dating to the first century BCE.

name: Anfiteatro minore
description: The so-called "Anfiteatro minore" is a small amphitheater at Puteoli dating to the early Imperial period.

name: Aquincum Civil Amphitheater
description: The Aquincum Civil Amphitheater is the smaller of two Roman amphitheaters located in Budapest, Hungary. It was built between ca. A.D. 250 and 300.

name: Aquincum Military Amphitheater
description: The Aquincum Military Amphitheater dates to the reign of Antoninus Pius.

name: Arènes de Cimiez
description: A Roman amphitheater of the second century that was enlarged during the course of the third, the so-called Arènes de Cimiez measures 67 by 56 meters.

name: Arènes de Fréjus
description: A 12,000 seat Roman amphitheater located in Fréjus, France.

name: Arènes de Lutèce
description: The Roman amphitheater of Lutetia (modern Paris).

name: Arènes de Nîmes
description: The Arènes de Nîmes is a Roman amphitheater built at the end of the first century.

name: Arles Amphitheatre
description: The Arles Roman amphitheater or Arènes d'Arles was built ca. A.D. 90 and could seat over 20,000 spectators.

name: Arnières-sur-Iton
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B4 Arnières-sur-Iton

name: Arnsburg
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 12 B2 Arnsburg

name: Augusta Bagiennorum amphitheater
description: The Roman amphitheater of Augusta Bagiennorum.

name: Avenches Roman amphitheater
description: The Avenches Roman amphitheater.

name: Avitta Bibba amphitheater
description: A Roman amphitheater located in the modern Siliana Governorate of Tunisia.

name: Aydıncık Theater or Odeon
description: Remains of a small theater or odeon at ancient Kelenderis.

name: Beaumont-sur-Oise amphitheater
description: The Beaumont-sur-Oise Gallo-Roman amphitheater.

name: Bizye Roman amphitheater
description: A Roman amphitheater in eastern Thrace.

name: Bobadela Roman amphitheater
description: The Bobadela Roman amphitheater dates to the second century.

name: Caerleon Roman amphitheatre
description: Amphitheatre of the legionary fortress at Caerleon

name: Cáparra amphitheater
description: The Roman amphitheater at Cáparra (Oliva de Plasencia, Spain).

name: Castelnau-Montratier
description: A Gallo-Roman site located in the Barguelonne valley that covers some 4,000 square meters. Excavation (1962-1964) suggested the site was likely a rural sanctuary that included a theater and baths.

name: Chassenon Roman amphitheater
description: Remains of a Roman amphitheater at Chassenon.

name: Civitas Camunnorum amphitheater
description: The ruins of a Roman amphitheater.

name: Conímbriga amphitheatre
description: The Roman amphitheater at Conímbriga.

name: Corinth Theater
description: The origins of the theater at Corinth lie in the fifth century B.C. The complex was further expanded during the Hellenistic period. An elaborate renovation during the reign of Hadrian further improved the complex and added a new orchestra and scaenae frons.

name: Crypta Balbi
description: The Crypta Balbi, mentioned by name only in the Regionary Catalogues, most likely refers to a vaulted passageway built in the Campus Martius by the proconsul Lucius Cornelius Balbus in 15 BC, who also dedicated a theater there in the same year.

name: Cyaneae Theater
description: The Cyaneae Theater dates to the Roman imperial period.

name: Drobeta amphitheater
description: The Drobeta Roman amphitheater.

name: Eastern theater at Laodicea ad Lycum
description: Digitized by hand in Google Earth in July 2012 at an eye altitude of 683m. Dates are general for the settlement, following Foss and Mitchell in BAtlas 65.

name: Ekklesiasterion/Theater at Metapontum
description: A building complex located in the city's agora, the Ekklesiasterion/Theater is dated ca. 625-300 B.C.

name: Euonymos Theater
description: A fourth-century BCE theater at Euonymos with a rectangular orchestra.

name: Fabrateria Nova amphitheater
description: Remains of a Roman amphitheater at Fabrateria Nova.

name: Falerii Novi amphitheater
description: The Falerii Novi amphitheater is located north of the city walls.

name: Flavian Amphitheater at Puteoli
description: The Flavian Amphitheater at Puteoli is an amphitheater constructed in the port town of Puteoli under the Flavian dynasty around 80 CE.

name: Gallo-Roman amphitheater at Grannum
description: The Gallo-Roman amphitheater at Grannum dates to the late first century B.C.

name: Gallo-Roman amphitheater at Montbouy
description: The Gallo-Roman amphitheater at Montbouy dates to the early Imperial period.

name: Gallo-Roman Amphitheater of Agen
description: A Gallo-Roman amphitheater dating to the early third century CE.

name: Gallo-Roman amphitheater-theater of Gennes
description: A theater / amphitheater dating to the early second century.

name: Gallo-Roman theater at Alba Helviorum
description: A third and fourth century theater at Alba Helviorum.

name: Gallo-Roman theater at Dalheim
description: The Gallo-Roman theater at Dalheim.

name: Gallo-Roman Theater of Lillebonne
description: The Gallo-Roman Theater of Lillebonne was in use from the first to the third century.

name: Gallo-Roman theater of Mandeure
description: The Roman theater of Mandeure dates to the first century CE.

name: Gemellae
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 34 D3 Gemellae

name: Graeco-Roman theater at Isthmia
description: A theater founded ca. 390 B.C. with subsequent Hellenistic and Roman renovations.

name: Graeco-Roman theater at Marina di Gioiosa Ionica
description: A second-century B.C. theater that represents a point of architectural transition from the Greek-style theater building to the Roman typology.

name: Graeco-Roman theater at Oinoanda
description: The theater at Oinoanda is believed to date from the second century B.C. It sits on the northern fringe of the city.

name: Greek Theater
description: Remains of a mud-brick theater with building inscription in Greek were excavated at Babylon. The structure was substantively rebuilt in modern times.

name: Greek theater at Alabanda
description: The Greek theater at Alabanda dates to the Hellenistic period.

name: Greek Theater at Hadrian's Villa
description: The Greek Theater at Hadrian's Villa in Tivoli.

name: Greek Theater at Syracuse
description: Rock-cut theater of Greek form at Syracuse, Sicily, probably in use from the 5th c. BC to the Late Roman period.

name: Greek Theater of Antiphellos
description: The Greek Theater of Antiphellos dates to the Hellenistic period.

name: Greek theater of Morgantina
description: The Greek theater of Morgantina was built during the third century B.C.

name: Greek theater of Segesta
description: Remains of a Greek theater at Segesta that was first built in the first half of the third century B.C.

name: Greek theater of Taormina
description: The Greek theater of Taormina was built originally in the third century B.C.

name: Greek theater of Tyndaris
description: The Greek theater of Tyndaris dates originally to the fourth century B.C.

name: Hellenistic theater at Dion
description: The heavily reconstructed remains of the Hellenistic theater at Dion. Parts of the ancient underground stage system are preserved.

name: Hellenistic-Roman theater at Sarno
description: An ancient theater.

name: Il Tondo
description: A first-century AD Roman amphitheater located in Latium near Zagorolo, Italy.

name: Il Tondo
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 43 D2 Il Tondo

name: Khamissa Roman Theater
description: A Roman theater at Thubursicum Numidarum (modern Khamissa in Algeria), first published by European scholars in the mid-19th century.

name: Künzing Roman amphitheater
description: A Roman timber amphitheater dating from the first to fifth centuries, the Künzing Roman amphitheater measured 46 by 40 meters. The site was discovered during excavations carried out in 2003 and 2004. The archaeological site is no longer visible but a reconstructed evocation of the ancient structure marks the location.

name: Lambaesis amphitheater
description: The Roman amphitheater at Lambaesis.

name: Large Theater
description: The so-called "Large Theater" at Pompeii was built in the Greek style during the second century B.C. Its capacity was approximately 5,000 spectators.

name: Larinum Roman amphitheater
description: An amphitheater of the first century A.D.

name: Legio VII Gemina
description: The Roman Legio VII Gemina was based in Tarraconensis and gave its name to the settlement. This ancient place is now León, Spain, which is built atop the ancient settlement.

name: Lepti Minus Roman amphitheater
description: The Roman amphitheater of Lepti Minus.

name: Magarsus Theater
description: The ancient theater at Magarsus.

name: Magnesia Theater 1
description: Renovated during the early Roman imperial period, this theater at Magnesia on the Maeander was located close to the city's agora.

name: Magnesia Theater 2
description: One of two theaters found at Magnesia on the Maeander, this so-called "theatron" was built in the late Hellenistic or early Imperial period and was destroyed by a landslide in antiquity.

name: Malo rimsko kazalište (Roman theater)
description: The Roman theater in Pula dates to the first century.

name: Meninx amphitheater
description: The Roman amphitheater of Meninx.

name: Mieza theater
description: The theater at Mieza dates from the second century BCE.

name: North Theater (Jerash)
description: The so-called North Theater at Jerash is also referred to as an Odeion. It dates ca. 165 CE.

name: Odeion at Sagalassos
description: The Odeion at Sagalassos occupies a terrace that is north of the Lower Agora and downslope from the Upper Agora.

name: Odeon
description: The Odeon (VIII, 7, 19) is a small roofed theater in Pompeii. It was built between 80 and 75 BC, under commission by two public officials, C. Quinctius Valgus and Marcus Porcius.

name: Odéon at Carthage
description: The theater and odéon of Roman Carthage stood in the northeast of the city, some three blocks from the cardo maximus.

name: Odeon at Hadrian's Villa
description: The Odeon at Hadrian's Villa is also referred to as the South Theater. It is located at the southern end of the Villa Adriana.

name: Odeon at Kom el-Dikka
description: The Odeon at Kom el-Dikka, used both for entertainment and academic functions, was built in the center of ancient Alexandria in the fourth century CE and rebuilt between the end of the fifth and beginning of the sixth century CE.

name: Orchestra in the Agora of Athens
description: A location for festival performances in the Agora of Athens, known solely from literary sources and perhaps also called "the grandstands" (ta ikria). Performances were transferred to the Theater of Dionysos on the south slope of the Acropolis in the 490s BC, but the name remained in use for a location in the Agora.

name: Périgueux Roman amphitheater
description: The Périgueux Roman amphitheater dates to the first century.

name: Pollenzo Roman amphitheater
description: The Roman amphitheater at ancient Pollentia (modern Pollenzo).

name: Pula Arena (Roman amphitheater)
description: The Pula Arena is a Roman amphitheater dating to the first century.

name: Rhodopolis Ancient Theater
description: The ancient theater at Rhodopolis.

name: Roman amphitheater
description: The Roman amphitheater is located on the opposite bank of the wadi from Bararus.

name: Roman amphitheater at *Moridunum
description: The Roman amphitheater at Moridunum is located in Carmarthenshire. It is one of only seven surviving Roman amphitheaters in Britain.

name: Roman amphitheater at Abella
description: The Roman amphitheater at Abella has been compared to the amphitheater of Pompeii.

name: Roman amphitheater at Al(l)ifae
description: Remains of a Roman amphitheater dating to the later first century A.D.

name: Roman amphitheater at Alalie/Aleria
description: A Roman amphitheater located in eastern Corsica.

name: Roman amphitheater at Albano Laziale
description: The Roman amphitheater at Albano Laziale is generally dated to the mid-third century A.D.

name: Roman amphitheater at Album Ingaunum/Albingaunum
description: The Roman amphitheater at Albegna.

name: Roman amphitheater at Amiternum
description: A Roman amphitheater dating to the first century A.D.

name: Roman amphitheater at Ancona
description: The Roman amphitheater at Ancona could accommodate 8,000 to 10,000 spectators.

name: Roman amphitheater at Aosta
description: The Roman amphitheater at Aosta likely dates to the first century A.D. Its remains are englobed within Medieval structures.

name: Roman amphitheater at Ariminum
description: The Roman amphitheater at Ariminum is dated to the second century, perhaps during the reign of Hadrian.

name: Roman amphitheater at Arretium
description: The Roman amphitheater at Arretium was built during the late first and early second centuries. It measures 121 by 68 meters.

name: Roman amphitheater at Augusta Raurica
description: The Roman amphitheater at Augusta Raurica accommodated ca. 13,000 spectators and was built ca. A.D. 170.

name: Roman amphitheater at Augustomagus
description: A small, first-century amphitheater with an ellipsoidal plan.

name: Roman amphitheater at Bern
description: The remains of a small Roman amphitheater at Bern, measuring ca. 27.5 x 25.3 meters, are visible on satellite images.

name: Roman amphitheater at Beth Govrin/Eleutheropolis
description: A Roman amphitheater at Beth Govrin/Eleutheropolis dating after the Jewish revolt of the first century A.D.

name: Roman amphitheater at Béziers
description: The Roman amphitheater at Béziers dates ca. A.D. 80, during the Flavian period. It measured ca. 110 by 90 meters and could accommodate 15,000 spectators.

name: Roman amphitheater at Bulla Regia
description: The Roman amphitheater at Bulla Regia

name: Roman amphitheater at Burdigala
description: The so-called "Palais Gallien" is an early second century A.D. amphitheater.

name: Roman amphitheater at Burnum
description: A Roman amphitheater associated with the legionary establishment of Burnum.

name: Roman amphitheater at Calleva Atrebatum
description: The Roman amphitheater at Calleva Atrebatum began as a timber structure ca. A.D. 55-75. Its remains lie north of the east gate of the third century A.D. town wall and the site was first identified by William Stukeley in 1724. The amphitheater measures approximately 90 by 75 meters.

name: Roman amphitheater at Caralis
description: The Roman amphitheater at Cagliari dates to the first and second centuries.

name: Roman amphitheater at Carmo
description: The Roman amphitheater at Carmo could accommodate 18,000 spectators.

name: Roman amphitheater at Carsulae
description: The Roman amphitheater at Carsulae dates to the first century A.D.

name: Roman amphitheater at Carthago Nova/Col. Urbs Iulia
description: The Roman amphitheater at Cartagena dates to the first century.

name: Roman amphitheater at Casinum
description: The Roman amphitheater at Casinum was built thanks to the patronage of Ummidia Quadratilla in the first century.

name: Roman amphitheater at Corinth
description: The Roman amphitheater at Corinth was located northeast of the Temple of Apollo. It has been suggested that it dates to the early days of the Roman colony, perhaps placing its construction in the first century B.C.

name: Roman amphitheater at Cumae
description: A Roman amphitheater at Cumae in Campania.

name: Roman amphitheater at Cyzicus
description: The Roman amphitheater at Cyzicus.

name: Roman amphitheater at Deva
description: The second-century A.D. Roman amphitheater at Deva Victrix (modern Chester, England) was the largest military amphitheater in Britain.

name: Roman amphitheater at Durno(no)varia
description: The Neolithic henge known as Maumbury Rings was adapted for use as an amphitheater space during the Roman period.

name: Roman amphitheater at Egnatia/Gnathia
description: The so-called Roman amphitheater at Egnatia/Gnathia dates to the Iulio-Claudian period. It is an elliptical structure with walls in opus Africanum that were revetted with painted plaster.

name: Roman amphitheater at Emporiae
description: The Roman amphitheater at Emporiae developed during the second century. It is located outside the city walls.

name: Roman amphitheater at Eporedia
description: The extramural Roman amphitheater at Eporedia is evidence of the expanding suburbium of the Roman settlement.

name: Roman amphitheater at Falerio Picenus
description: The Roman amphitheater at Falerio Picenus dates to the first century A.D.

name: Roman amphitheater at Flavia Solva
description: The Roman amphitheater of Flavia Solva.

name: Roman amphitheater at Florentia
description: The Roman amphitheater at Florentia was built between 124 and 130.

name: Roman amphitheater at Frusino
description: The Roman amphitheater at Frusino measures ca. 78 by 57 meters.

name: Roman amphitheater at Grumentum
description: Grumentum's amphitheater (first century B.C.) is the only known example in Lucania. The arena measures ca. 62.5 by 60 meters.

name: Roman amphitheater at Guildhall, London
description: The Roman amphitheater of London, now beneath the Guildhall Art Gallery, was discovered in 1988.

name: Roman amphitheater at Hispellum
description: The Roman amphitheater at Hispellum dates to the first century.

name: Roman amphitheater at Hydata Hypsitana/Forum Traiani
description: The Roman amphitheater at Forum Traiani, modern Fordongianus

name: Roman amphitheater at Interamnia Praetuttiorum
description: A Roman amphitheater of the first century A.D.

name: Roman amphitheater at Iol Caesarea
description: The Roman amphitheater at Iol Caesarea was of moderate size, with room for about 16,000 spectators.

name: Roman amphitheater at Lepcis Magna
description: The Roman amphitheater at Lepcis Magna dates to the first century A.D. and was dedicated to Nero.

name: Roman amphitheater at Libarna
description: The remains of the Roman amphitheater at Libarna are located in Serravalle Scrivia, Italy.

name: Roman amphitheater at Lixus
description: The ruins of a Roman amphitheater at Lixus, one of only a few such structures known in Berber North Africa.

name: Roman amphitheater at Luca
description: The Roman amphitheater at Luca dates to the second half of the first century.

name: Roman amphitheater at Lucera
description: An amphitheater dating to the time of Augustus.

name: Roman amphitheater at Lucus Feroniae
description: The Roman amphitheater at Lucus Feroniae was financed by one M. Silius Epaphroditus, who in turn received the title patronus. The structure dates to the reign of Tiberius.

name: Roman amphitheater at Luna
description: The Roman amphitheater at Luna was built in the second century, perhaps during the Antonine period.

name: Roman amphitheater at Lupia(e)
description: The Roman amphitheater at Lupia(e), modern Lecce, was built in the late first and early second centuries A.D.

name: Roman amphitheater at Mactaris
description: The Roman amphitheater at Mactaris dates to the second century.

name: Roman amphitheater at Marruvium
description: The Roman amphitheater at Marruvium measures 95 by 75 meters.

name: Roman Amphitheater at Mérida
description: The Amphitheatre of Mérida was inaugurated in 8 B.C.

name: Roman amphitheater at Noviodunum
description: The Roman amphitheater at Noviodunum was only discovered in 1996 and likely dates to the second century A.D. The arena measures ca. 50 x 36 meters.

name: Roman amphitheater at Nuceria (Alfaterna)
description: The Roman amphitheater at Nuceria (Alfaterna) likely dates to the Augustan period.

name: Roman amphitheater at Ocriculum
description: The Roman amphitheater at Ocriculum lies outside the city walls.

name: Roman amphitheater at Paestum
description: The Roman amphitheater at Paestum was built ca. 50 B.C., making it one of the earliest extant amphitheaters.

name: Roman amphitheater at Patavium
description: The Roman amphitheater at Patavium dates to the Augustan period.

name: Roman amphitheater at Porolissum
description: An amphitheater that originated under Hadrian and that was then rebuilt in stone ca. A.D. 157.

name: Roman amphitheater at Ptolemais/Barkes Limen
description: The Roman amphitheater at Ptolemais/Barkes Limen

name: Roman amphitheater at Richborough
description: The earthwork remains of the Roman amphitheater associated with the Saxon Shore fort at Richborough.

name: Roman amphitheater at Rudiae
description: The Roman amphitheater at Rudiae is part of the complement of Roman buildings dating to the imperial period.

name: Roman amphitheater at Rusellae
description: The Roman amphitheater at Rusellae dates to the first century.

name: Roman amphitheater at Salona
description: The Roman amphitheater at Salona.

name: Roman amphitheater at Santa Maria Capua Vetere
description: Sometimes referred to as the "Anfiteatro Campano", the Roman amphitheater at Santa Maria Capua Vetere is the second largest in the Roman world. Begun under Augustus, it was further renovated under Hadrian and Antoninus Pius.

name: Roman amphitheater at Scylletium/Scolacium
description: The Roman amphitheater at Scylletium/Scolacium.

name: Roman amphitheater at Scythopolis/Nysa
description: The Roman amphitheater at Scythopolis/Nysa.

name: Roman amphitheater at Segobriga
description: The Roman amphitheater at Segobriga dates to the Flavian period. It measures approximately 75 by 65 meters and could seat 5,500 spectators.

name: Roman amphitheater at Simitthu
description: The poorly preserved remains of an amphitheater that had likely been abandoned by the fifth century.

name: Roman amphitheater at Sipous/Sipontum
description: The Roman amphitheater at Sipous/Sipontum.

name: Roman amphitheater at Sopron
description: The Roman amphitheater at Sopron dates to the later first century A.D.

name: Roman amphitheater at Spoletium
description: The remains of the early Imperial amphitheater were discovered within the Caserma Minervio during the early nineteenth century.

name: Roman amphitheater at Suasa
description: The Roman amphitheater at Suasa dates to the first century A.D.

name: Roman amphitheater at Sutri
description: The Roman amphitheater at Sutri likely dates to the reign of Augustus.

name: Roman Amphitheater at Syracuse
description: A Roman Amphitheater at Syracuse, Sicily, that was in use from the first through third centuries. The structure measured ca. 140 x 119 meters.

name: Roman amphitheater at Teate
description: The Roman amphitheater at Teate dates to the first century.

name: Roman amphitheater at Telesia
description: A Late Republican Roman amphitheater at Telesia.

name: Roman amphitheater at Thermae Himeraeae
description: Roman amphitheater at Termini Imerese was of modest size and is today poorly preserved.

name: Roman amphitheater at Theveste
description: The Roman amphitheater at Theveste dates to the fourth century.

name: Roman amphitheater at Thibiuca
description: A Roman amphitheater located at ancient Thibiuca (modern Henchir Bou Cha).

name: Roman amphitheater at Thuburbo Maius
description: The Roman amphitheater at Thuburbo Maius is an extramural structure constructed by hollowing out elevated terrain.

name: Roman amphitheater at Tipasa
description: A Roman amphitheater at Tipasa.

name: Roman amphitheater at Tolosa
description: The Roman amphitheater at Purpan-Ancely dates ca. A.D. 40 and is the only significant Roman architecture to survive from ancient Tolosa.

name: Roman amphitheater at Trebula
description: The Roman amphitheater at Trebula dates to the Augustan period.

name: Roman amphitheater at Trebula Mutuesca
description: The archaeological remains of the Roman amphitheater at Trebula Mutuesca are located at Monteleone Sabino in the Province of Rieti, Italy.

name: Roman amphitheater at Trier
description: The Roman amphitheater at Trier dates to the reign of Antoninus Pius and was renovated under Constantius Chlorus. The arena measures ca. 71 x 47.5 meters.

name: Roman amphitheater at Tusculum
description: Today the Roman amphitheater at Tusculum is poorly preserved. These ruins have never been systematically investigated.

name: Roman amphitheater at Uchi Maius
description: A Roman amphitheater located at Uchi Maius (modern Henchir ed-Douâmis).

name: Roman amphitheater at Urbs Salvia
description: The Roman amphitheater at Urbs Salvia.

name: Roman amphitheater at Uthina
description: The Roman amphitheater at Uthina dates to the reign of Hadrian and seated about 16,000 spectators.

name: Roman amphitheater at Veleia
description: The Roman amphitheater of Roman Veleia.

name: Roman amphitheater at Vercellae
description: An extramural Roman amphitheater was to be found at Vercellae.

name: Roman amphitheater at Verona
description: The Roman amphitheater at Verona dates to ca. A.D. 30.

name: Roman amphitheater at Viminacium
description: The Roman amphitheater at Viminacium could accommodate roughly 12,000 spectators.

name: Roman amphitheater at Vindonissa
description: The Roman amphitheater at Vindonissa was built under the reign of Tiberius.

name: Roman amphitheater at Volaterrae
description: The archaeological remains of an amphitheater were discovered at Volterra in 2015. Preliminary work suggests that the structure measured ca. 70 x 90 meters and had three tiers of seats that could accommodate 8,000 to 10,000 spectators.

name: Roman amphitheater at Volsinii
description: The Roman amphitheater at Volsinii dates to the second half of the first century A.D. It measures ca. 86 x 69 meters.

name: Roman amphitheater of Asisium
description: In the Porta Perlici of Assisi are the remains of a first century A.D. Roman amphitheater.

name: Roman amphitheater of Beneventum
description: The Roman amphitheater of Beneventum dates to the reign of the emperor Nero.

name: Roman amphitheater of Carthage
description: A Roman amphitheater built at Carthage.

name: Roman amphitheater of Catania
description: The Roman amphitheater of Catania is the largest known ancient amphitheater in Sicily. Its external dimensions measure ca. 125 x 105 meters. It likely dates to the second century.

name: Roman amphitheater of Col. Tarraco
description: A Roman amphitheater in Tarragona built in the first century A.D. The structure was renovated under Elagabalus.

name: Roman amphitheater of Italica
description: The amphitheater of Italica, built in the second century A.D., was one of the largest in the Roman world and could accommodate an estimated 25,000 spectators.

name: Roman amphitheater of Martigny
description: The Roman amphitheater of Martigny likely dates to the reign of Trajan.

name: Roman amphitheater of Milan
description: The Roman amphitheater of Milan was built near the Porta Ticinese during the second and third centuries. It was destroyed by the time of the Gothic Wars, most likely during the fifth century.

name: Roman amphitheater of Saintes
description: The Gallo-Roman amphitheater of Mediolanum Santonum.

name: Roman Amphitheater of Serdica
description: The Roman Amphitheater of Serdica was built during the third and fourth centuries CE atop remains of an earlier theater complex. The complex was abandoned by the fifth century CE. The site was rediscovered beginning in 2004.

name: Roman amphitheater of Thyna
description: A Roman amphitheater located at Thaenea, a Roman station in the province of Africa Proconsularis.

name: Roman amphitheater of Tours
description: A Roman amphitheater at Tours that dates to the first century CE.

name: Roman amphitheater of Venusia
description: A Roman amphitheater dating to the first and second centuries.

name: Roman amphitheater of Virunum
description: The Virunum Roman amphitheater is located in Maria Saal, Austria.

name: Roman amphitheater, Córdoba
description: The Roman amphitheater of Córdoba.

name: Roman amphitheatre, Charterhouse
description: An amphitheater stood to the west of the lead mining settlement at Charterhouse Roman Town.

name: Roman civil amphitheater at Carnuntum
description: Located outside the city wall, the Roman amphitheater at Carnuntum dates to the later second century. It could accommodate some 13,000 spectators. The amphitheater likely went out of use by the fourth century.

name: Roman macellum at Aeclanum
description: Once hypothetically identified by some scholars as an amphitheater, this structure at Aeclanum is now understood to be a Roman macellum.

name: Roman Odeion at Corinth
description: The Roman Odeion at Corinth dates to the first century A.D.

name: Roman Odeon of Gortyn
description: The Roman Odeon of Gortyn dates to the first century.

name: Roman odeon, Vienne
description: The Roman odeon at Vienne.

name: Roman theater
description: The theater at Thugga dates to 168 or 169 and could accommodate 3500 spectators.

name: Roman theater (Haidra)
description: The remains of a Roman theater located at Haidra, Tunisia.

name: Roman theater at [Augusta] Ambianorum
description: The Roman theater lies to the east of the ancient settlement.

name: Roman theater at Aizanoi
description: The Roman theater at Aizanoi is part of a theater-stadium complex. The complex is built after A.D. 160 with the patronage of one M. Apuleius Eurycles.

name: Roman theater at Amiternum
description: The Roman theater at Amiternum could seat approximately 2,000 people. It was abandoned after the fourth century and became a necropolis.

name: Roman theater at Amman/Philadelpheia
description: A second century Roman theater at Amman that could seat 6,000 spectators.

name: Roman theater at Antium
description: The Roman theater at Antium is most likely built in the second half of the first century.

name: Roman theater at Aosta
description: The Roman theater at Aosta dates ca. 25 B.C.

name: Roman theater at Asculum
description: The Roman theater at Asculum was discovered during excavations in 1932. The structure dates to the first century A.D.

name: Roman theater at Augusta Bagiennorum
description: The Roman theater at Augusta Bagiennorum

name: Roman theater at Benevento
description: A Roman theater of the second century A.D. constructed during the reign of Hadrian.

name: Roman Theater at Bosra
description: The Roman Theater at Bosra dates to the second century A.D.

name: Roman theater at Brixia
description: The Roman theater at Brixia was built during the Flavian period.

name: Roman theater at Cádiz
description: The Roman theater at Cádiz was one of the largest in the Roman world, accommodating some 20,000 spectators. It dates originally to the first century B.C. In the thirteenth century it was incorporated into a fortress built by King Alfonso X of Castile.

name: Roman theater at Cales
description: The Roman theater at Cales dates to the first century B.C.

name: Roman theater at Carsulae
description: The Roman theater at Carsulae dates to the Augustan period, pre-dating the amphitheater.

name: Roman theater at Carthage
description: The Roman theater at Carthage was built around the middle of the second century A.D. It was re-discovered in 1904.

name: Roman theater at Carthago Nova/Col. Urbs Iulia
description: The Roman theater at Cartagena dates to the very end of the first century B.C. and was dedicated to Caius and Lucius Caesar.

name: Roman theater at Casinum
description: The Roman theater at Casinum dates to the Augustan period.

name: Roman theater at Castrum Novum
description: The Roman theater at Castrum Novum (Santa Marinella, Italy).

name: Roman theater at Col. Augusta Raurica
description: The theater at Augusta Raurica is one of the best preserved Roman theaters north of the Alps.

name: Roman theater at Cuicul
description: The Roman theater at Cuicul was built during the reign of Antoninus Pius.

name: Roman theater at Falerii
description: The Roman theater at Falerii was excavated in 1829.

name: Roman theater at Falerio Picenus
description: The Roman theater at Falerio Picenus was completed during the reign of Tiberius.

name: Roman theater at Ferentinum
description: The Roman theater at Ferentinum is today poorly preserved.

name: Roman theater at Ferentium
description: The Roman theater at Ferentium dates to the early Imperial period.

name: Roman theater at Fiesole
description: The Roman theater at Fiesole dates to the first century.

name: Roman theater at Fréjus
description: The Roman theater at Fréjus dates to the reign of Tiberius.

name: Roman theater at Gortyna
description: The Roman theater at Gortyna dates to the second century.

name: Roman theater at Gosbecks
description: A Roman-British theater was excavated some 125 yards due south of the Gosbecks sanctuary.

name: Roman theater at Helikore/Nicaea
description: Pliny the Younger discussed the restoration of the theater in a letter sent to the Roman emperor Trajan.

name: Roman theater at Helvia Ricina
description: Helvia Ricina flourished under Augustus and Tiberius and its theater is the largest in Picenum.

name: Roman theater at Herakleia
description: The Roman theater at Herakleia dates to the time of Hadrian and remained in use until the fourth century. Its remains came to light in 1968.

name: Roman theater at Iguvium
description: The Roman theater at Iguvium is located within the Guastuglia archaeological area and dates to the late republican period. Sear (2004) notes several important architectural innovations evident in the theater's construction.

name: Roman theater at Interamnia Praetuttiorum
description: A Roman theater dating to the Augustan period.

name: Roman theater at Iol Caesarea
description: An important early example of Roman theatral architecture.

name: Roman theater at Italica
description: The Roman theater at Italica could accommodate 3,000 spectators. It predates the Hadrianic rebuilding of the city.

name: Roman theater at Iuvanum
description: The Roman theater at Iuvanum is located at the foot of the acropolis.

name: Roman theater at Lepcis Magna
description: The Roman theater at Lepcis Magna was financed in the first century by Annobal Tapapius Rufus.

name: Roman theater at Libarna
description: The Roman theater at Libarna dates to the first century.

name: Roman theater at Lupia(e)
description: The precise date of the Roman theater at Lecce is uncertain, but it is generally assigned to the Augustan period.

description: The remains of the first century B.C. Roman theater are located adjacent to the Alcazaba of Málaga. The theater is not completely excavated, although epigraphic data places it in the Augustan period. Its abandonment came in the third century.

name: Roman Theater at Mérida
description: The Roman Theatre of Mérida was sponsored by Marcus Agrippa and dates ca. 16-15 B.C.

name: Roman theater at Metellinum
description: The Roman theater at Metellinum (modern Medelin, Spain) dates to the late first century B.C.

name: Roman theater at Mogontiacum
description: The site of the Roman theater in Mainz.

name: Roman theater at Nicopolis
description: The Roman theater at Nicopolis incorporates both Greek and Roman architectural practices in its construction.

name: Roman theater at Ocriculum
description: The Roman theater at Ocriculum dates to the early imperial period.

name: Roman theater at Ostia
description: The Roman theater at Ostia was built under the patronage of Marcus Agrippa.

name: Roman theater at Pelusium
description: The Roman theater at Pelusium (Tell Farama).

name: Roman theater at Perge
description: The Roman theater at Perge was built ca. 120 AD and renovated ca. 250 AD.

name: Roman theater at Petra
description: The Roman theater at Petra.

name: Roman theater at Salamis/Konstantia
description: Salamis remained an important city of Cyprus during the Roman period and the theater is one part of a complex of monumental architecture there.

name: Roman theater at Salona
description: The Roman theater of Salona.

name: Roman Theater at Sepphoris
description: The ancient Roman Theater at Sepphoris.

name: Roman theater at Soloi/Aipeia?
description: The Roman theater at Soloi, Cyprus.

name: Roman theater at Sparta
description: The Roman theater at Sparta dates to the late first century B.C.

name: Roman theater at Spoletium
description: The Roman theater at Spoletium dates to the first century B.C.

description: The Roman theater at Suessa Aurunca was built in the first century A.D. and then restored under Antoninus Pius. Amedeo Maiuri initiated excavations at the theater site in the 1920s. The excavation was not completed until 2003.

name: Roman theater at Sufetula
description: The Roman theater at Sufetula.

name: Roman theater at Teate
description: The Roman theater at Teate measures 80 meters in diameter.

name: Roman theater at Tergeste
description: The Roman theater at Tergeste dates to the late first century A.D.

name: Roman theater at Terracina
description: The ancient Roman theater of Terracina dates from the first century BCE.

name: Roman theater at Thignica
description: The Roman theater at Thignica.

name: Roman theater at Tlos
description: The Roman theater at Tlos preserves 34 rows of seating.

name: Roman theater at Tusculum
description: The Roman theater at Tusculum was first excavated in 1839.

name: Roman theater at Utica
description: The Roman theater at Utica.

name: Roman theater at Vendeuil-Caply
description: The larger of the two Roman theaters at Vendeuil-Caply was located at the northwest angle of the cardo and the decumanus.

name: Roman theater at Verona
description: The Roman theater at Verona dates from the late first century B.C.

name: Roman theater at Volaterrae
description: The Roman theater of Volaterrae was built during the during the Augustan period under the consulship of Aulus Caecina Severus.

name: Roman theater of Acinipo
description: The Roman theater of Acinipo dates ca. 59-53 B.C.

name: Roman theater of Adraha
description: Ruins of a Roman theater located at modern Dar'a in Syria.

name: Roman theater of Aspendos
description: The Roman theater at Aspendos dates ca. AD 155 and is perhaps the best preserved example of a Roman theater building.

name: Roman Theater of Augustodunum
description: The Roman theater of Augustodunum dates to the reign of Trajan and was dedicated in 105 CE.

name: Roman Theater of Baelo
description: A Roman theater in Baelo that dates to the reign of Claudius.

name: Roman theater of Catania
description: The Roman theater of Catania was built in the second century CE and remained in use until the fifth or sixth century. It could accommodate approximately 7,000 spectators.

name: Roman theater of Fourvière
description: A Roman theater in Lyon, France, that originally dates ca. 15 B.C.

name: Roman theater of Guelma
description: The Roman theater of ancient Calama (now Guelma) was built in the early third century CE and substantially restored in the twentieth century.

name: Roman theater of Herculaneum
description: The Roman theater of Herculaneum was constructed during the Augustan period. The theater was discovered first in 1709 while a well was being dug.

name: Roman Theater of Jableh
description: A Roman theater located at Gabala (Syria).

name: Roman theater of Lokroi Epizephyrioi
description: A Roman theater of the first century B.C. was built on the site of the fourth century B.C. Greek theater.

name: Roman theater of Milan
description: The Roman theater of Milan was built during the Augustan period and could accommodate 7,000 to 9,000 spectators.

name: Roman Theater of Minturnae
description: A theater constructed in Minturnae under Augustus or Tiberius, and further embellished under Hadrian.

name: Roman theater of Neapolis
description: The Roman theater of Neapolis is a structure of the first century B.C. that is built atop the pre-existing fourth century B.C. Greek theater.

name: Roman theater of Philippopolis
description: The Roman theater in Plovdiv, Bulgaria.

name: Roman theater of Privernum
description: The Roman theater of Privernum is attested mainly by means of epigraphic evidence.

name: Roman theater of Saguntum
description: The Roman theater of Saguntum could accommodate approximately 8,000 spectators.

name: Roman theater of Scythopolis
description: The Roman theater of Scythopolis.

name: Roman Theater of Side
description: The Roman Theater of Side dates to the second century CE.

name: Roman theater of Stobi
description: The Roman theater of Stobi dates to the second century.

name: Roman theater of Suasa
description: The Roman theater of Suasa was discovered in 2003 during excavations near the amphitheater. The semicircular structure measures ca. 55 meters in diameter and dates to the first century A.D.

name: Roman theater of Vienne
description: A first century Roman theater in Vienne.

name: Roman theater of Zaragoza
description: Dating to the first century, the Roman theater of Zaragoza accommodated about 6,000 spectators.

name: Roman theater, Atri
description: The Roman theater of Atri.

name: Roman theater, Jublains
description: The Roman theater at modern Jublains.

name: Roman theater, Singilia Barba
description: The Roman theater of Singilia Barba

name: Roman theater, Tipasa
description: The Roman theater at Tipasa.

name: Roman Theatre at Caere
description: The Roman theater at Caere (modern Cerveteri, Italy) is known especially for the sculptural program honoring Julio-Claudian emperors, including Tiberius and Claudius.

name: Roman Theatre of Orange
description: The Roman Theatre of Orange was built early in the first century A.D.

name: Sabratha amphitheater
description: The Sabratha amphitheater dates to the later second century.

name: Sabratha Roman Theater
description: A large Roman theater, likely built sometime in the third century CE.

name: San Roque Theatre - Carteia
description: The Roman theater of Carteia.

name: Sanctuary of Dionysos
description: The Sanctuary of Dionysos, which includes a theater, choregic monument, statue bases, and at least one temple, formed part of the civic center of the Attic deme of Ikarion.

name: Sapperton and Cirencester amphitheatre
description: The Cirencester amphitheater is the second largest known in Britannia. Its earthworks supported tiered seats that could accommodate about 8,000 spectators. The amphitheater was converted to a fortified site in the fifth century during the Saxon invasions.

name: Sarmizegetusa Roman amphitheater
description: The Roman amphitheater at Colonia Ulpia Traiana Augusta Dacica Sarmizegetusa was built in A.D. 108/110 and renovated in 158 under the legio IV Flavia Felix

name: Second ancient theater, Larisa
description: The second ancient theater at Larisa dates to the first century BCE.

name: Simitthu Roman theater
description: The Roman theater at Simitthu, an ancient Roman-Berber town in northwestern Tunisia.

name: Small Roman theater at Vendeuil-Caply
description: Site of the small Roman theater on Le Mont Châtelet.

name: Small Theater (Antipatris)
description: A small theater of the Roman era, located on the site of Antipatris. It may never have been completed, or it was heavily spoliated prior to its ultimate destruction in an earthquake in 363 CE. The original excavator tentatively suggested identifying it as an odeon, but for various archaeological and stylistic reasons, this view has not been widely accepted.

name: So-called Theatre 2
description: The ruins of an ancient theatre in Cyrene, known as "Theatre 2".

name: So-called Theatre 3
description: A Roman theatre on the acropolis ridge of Cyrene, conventionally numbered "3".

name: South Theater (Jerash)
description: The South Theater of Jerash in Jordan dates to the late first century CE.

name: St-André-sur-Cailly
description: An ancient place, cited: BAtlas 11 B3 St-André-sur-Cailly

name: St-Georges-du-Bois
description: St-Georges-du-Bois is the site of a Gallo-Roman amphitheater.

name: Studforth Hill
description: The remains of a Romano-British amphitheater were discovered on Studforth Hill, just outside the village of Aldborough between Harrogate and York in northern England.

name: Susa Roman amphitheater
description: The Roman amphitheater at Segusio.

name: Tavium Theater
description: The Roman theater of Tavium.

name: Teatro Clunia
description: A Roman theater initially constructed during the reign of Tiberius.

name: Teatro Romano de Regina
description: The Roman theater at Regina, now Badajoz, Spain.

name: Teatro romano, Aquino
description: The Roman theater of Aquinum.

name: The Theater of Larissa
description: The theater at Larissa was a large stone theater built around the end of the third century BC and used for dramatic performances and as a political meeting place.

name: Theater 1 (Telmessos)
description: An excavated ancient theater at Telmessos (Fethiye) has been variously dated to the late Hellenistic or Augustan periods. Some components (e.g., the extant stage structures) date to later in the Roman imperial period.

name: Theater 2 (Telmessos)
description: A small theater, located on the north slope of the acropolis at Telmessos.

name: Theater at Alinda/Alexandria ad Latmum
description: The Roman Theater at Alinda could accommodate about 5,000 spectators.

name: Theater at Arykanda
description: The Theater at Arykanda dates to the first century B.C.

name: Theater at Bodrum
description: The Theater at Bodrum is dedicated to Ares.

name: Theater at Butrint
description: A Greek and Roman theater at the site of Butrint.

name: Theater at Cillium
description: The Roman theater at Cillium.

name: Theater at Cyrrus/Hagioupolis
description: The Roman theater at Cyrrus dates ca. A.D. 100 - 220.

name: Theater at Delphi
description: The theater at Delphi near the Temple of Apollo.

name: Theater at Heraclea Minoa
description: The Theater at Heraclea Minoa dates to the late fourth century B.C.

name: Theater at Kibyra
description: The Theater at Kibyra dates to the Hellenistic period.

name: Theater at Letoon
description: An ancient theater associated with the Letoon, a sanctuary of Leto near the ancient city Xanthos in Lycia. The theater is located on the Roman agora, to the the north of the acropolis.

name: Theater at Leuke
description: The theater is located at the north-east end of Kouphonesi. It was a stone-built building that could accommodated ca. 1000 spectators.

name: Theater at Madauros
description: The Roman theater at Madauros in Africa Proconsularis.

name: Theater at Patara/Arsinoe
description: The Theater at Patara/Arsinoe dates to the reign of Tiberius and was subsequently renovated under the Antonines.

name: Theater at Pausilipum
description: A small Roman theater with a capacity of approximately 2,000 spectators.

name: Theater at Philippi
description: The initial phase of the Theater at Philippi dates to just after 356 B.C. when Philip II conquered the city.

name: Theater at Phoinike
description: The Greek Theater at Phoinike in Epirus.

name: Theater at Saepinum
description: The intramural theater at Saepinum is one of the best preserved structures at the site. Located in the northern part of the walled area, it is likely built between 2. B.C. and A.D. 4.

name: Theater at Segobriga
description: Construction of the Roman Theater at Segobriga began under Tiberius and was completed during the Flavian period.

name: Theater at Stymphalos
description: The remains of a rock-cut theater at Stymphalos.

name: Theater at Sybaris/Thurii/Copia
description: The Roman Theater at Sybaris/Thurii/Copia dates originally to the Augustan period.

name: Theater at Termessos
description: The Theater at Termessos is a Hellenistic and Roman structure, with the Roman theater preserving elements of the earlier phase.

name: Theater at Termessos
description: The ancient Theater at Termessos.

name: Theater at the Sanctuary of Asklepios, Epidauros
description: The Greek theater in the Sanctuary of Asklepios at Epidauros, renowned for its acoustics

name: Theater at Thorikos
description: The largely preserved classical theater at Thorikos is dated ca. 525-480 B.C.

name: Theater at Xanthos
description: The Greek theater (second century BC) was renovated following a Roman design following the earthquake of A.D. 141.

name: Theater of ancient Sicyon
description: The Theater of ancient Sicyon dates to either the late fourth or early third century BC. It was restructured after 146 B.C.

name: Theater of Aphrodisias
description: The Theater of Aphrodisias dates to the second half of the first century B.C. and stands on the eastern slope of the acropolis.

name: Theater of Assuras
description: The Roman theater of Assuras (Zanfour, Tunisia).

name: Theater of Cyrene
description: The Greek theater of Cyrene was constructed in the sixth century BCE and transformed into a Roman amphitheater in the second century CE.

name: Theater of Delos
description: An ancient theater located in the settlement of Delos.

name: Theater of Dionysus Eleuthereus
description: Theatre on the south slope of the Acropolis in Athens.

name: Theater of Ephesus
description: The Theater of Ephesus sits at the foot of the Panayir mountains. It could host up to 25,000 spectators.

name: Theater of Hippo Regius
description: The Theater of Hippo Regius is perhaps the largest Roman theater in North Africa. It was built in the second century CE.

name: Theater of Melos
description: The Theater of Melos likely has Hellenistic origins but the visible phase belongs to the Roman period.

name: Theater of Miletus
description: The Theater of Miletus was built in four phases between ca. 300 and ca. 133 B.C.

name: theater of Nysa
description: The ancient theater of Nysa could accommodate ca. 12,000 spectators.

name: Theater of Orchomenus
description: A theater of the late fourth century B.C.

name: theater of Peltuinum
description: The Roman theater of Peltuinum.

name: Theater of Pergamon
description: The Greek Theater of Pergamon dates ca. 225 -200 B.C.

name: Theater of Priene
description: Greek theater just below the agora in the Ionian city of Priene.

name: Theater of Pyrrhus
description: The Hellenistic theater at Dodona is linked to the reign of Pyrrhus in the third century B.C.

name: Theater of Sagalassos
description: The Theater of Sagalassos dates to the later second century.

name: Theater of Stratos
description: The Theater of Stratos dates to the late fourth century B.C.

name: Theater of Tegea
description: The remains of the Hellenistic theater of Tegea are to be found beneath the tenth-century A.D. Middle Byzantine church of Palaia Episkopi.

name: Theater of the Muses
description: A third- to second-century B.C. theater connected with the sanctuary of the Muses.

name: Theater of Tigranocerta
description: Theatre, Tigranocerta, Arzen

name: Theater of Tios
description: The ancient theater of Tios.

name: Theater of Zea
description: Visible remains of a Hellenistic theater located to the west of the Zea Harbor in Piraeus, dating ca. 200 B.C.

name: Theater of Zeugma
description: The remains of the ancient theater at Zeugma

name: Theater-temple complex, Monte San Nicola
description: A theater-temple complex that stands within pre-Roman walls belonging to a period of Samnite occupation on Monte San Nicola in the municipality of Pietravairano.

name: Théâtre antique de Tarquimpol
description: The ancient theater of Tarquimpol is located at the ancient settlement of Decempagi, present-day Tarquimpol, in the Moselle department. The theater was revealed by aerial photography in 1981; the theater measures approximately 117 meters in diameter and could accommodate up to 16,000 people.

name: Théâtre Antique de Vaison-la-Romaine
description: The Roman Theater of Vasio Vocontiorum.

name: Théatre Gallo-Romains
description: The so-called Théatre Gallo-Romains could accommodate some 6,600 spectators.

name: Theatre of Apollonia
description: The theatre of the ancient city of Apollonia, situated just outside the city wall, on the east side. The construction appears to be late Hellenistic, with half of the seats cut out of the rock on the elevation of the acropolis, and the other half built up.

name: Theatre of Messene
description: The ancient Theater of Messene (Mavrommati, Messenia, Greece).

name: Theatre of Palmyra
description: Theatre in the Ancient City of Palmyra.

name: Theatre of Parium
description: A Greek theatre at Parium that was reconstructed by the Romans in the second and early third century.

name: Theatrum Pompei
description: Rome's first permanent theater, located in the Campus Martius. The complex was completed under the second consulship of Pompeius Magnus in 55 B.C.

name: Thignica amphitheater
description: A Roman amphitheater at Thignica.

name: Tomen y Mur amphitheatre
description: An oval, embanked area of the north-east of the Roman fort has been identified as an amphitheater, one of only three known in Wales. The amphitheater is located near the site of the parade ground.

name: Tralles ancient theater
description: The theater of ancient Tralles.

name: Uluzibbira Roman amphitheater
description: A Roman amphitheater located in the modern Essed-Nord village, west of Sidi Bou Ali in Tunisia's Sousse governorate.

name: Urso theater
description: The Roman theater of Urso.

name: Verulamium Roman theater
description: The Roman Theatre at Verulamium, St. Albans was begun around the middle of the second century and continues in use until the fourth century.

name: Yalvac Theatre - Antioch Pisidia
description: The theater at Pisidian Antioch was perhaps founded in the first century BCE.
